concerns: SUPERSET VERDICT: NOT a strict superset. Combined calls 5 of the 7 module compute_*/create_* entry points: compute_portfolio_analysis, compute_factor_analysis, compute_optimization_analysis, compute_validation_analysis, compute_monte_carlo_analysis (combined_report l100-124). Standalones that exist as create_* but are NOT included: there is no standalone 'rebalancing report' and the legacy monte_carlo.py.create_monte_carlo_report (the spaghetti-paths version) is NOT used. For the five that ARE included, combined surfaces the full section list of each — so for those five it is a faithful superset.

GAP 1 — Rebalancing is plumbed but NEVER consumed. create_combined_report accepts rebalance_freq (l61), passes it to build_context (l85), and ctx stores it (report_context l40/181), but NO compute_* function reads ctx.rebalance_freq. rebalancing.simulate_rebalanced_portfolio and opt_plotting.plot_rebalancing_history are never called by any report. Every Growth-of-$1 curve uses get_portfolio_price (static buy-and-hold). So requesting a rebalance frequency silently changes nothing in any output.

GAP 2 — Monte Carlo parameters not exposed. compute_monte_carlo_analysis supports num_simulations/time_horizon/initial_investment (monte_carlo_report l187), and the STANDALONE create_monte_carlo_report forwards them from kwargs (l248-256), but the combined orchestrator calls it with NO args (combined_report l124), so the combined MC is locked to 5000 paths / 252 days / $10,000 regardless of what the caller wants. Also combined never passes an actual_return/actual_path, so MC 'Realized Percentile' / actual overlay (available in the legacy monte_carlo.py standalone) is absent.

GAP 3 — Silent module drop. Each delegate is wrapped in try/except that only logger.error/extend-skips (combined_report l99-126). If e.g. optimization or factor fetch fails, that ENTIRE section silently vanishes from the combined report with no user-visible note, whereas a standalone run would raise. This makes the combined report's completeness non-deterministic vs the standalones.
GAP 4 — desc=False parameter (combined_report l61) is accepted but never used anywhere in the function body — dead kwarg.

GAP 5 — Brinson uses EQUAL benchmark weights as a fallback (factor_report l219) rather than true benchmark constituents; and the factor section is entirely skipped if both Fama-French fetch fails AND sector_map is missing (factor_report l256-257), replaced by a stub 'Insufficient data' section. Combined inherits this.

Heads-up (not combined-specific): the combined report imports ONE ctx and threads it everywhere, which is the right move, but the per-strategy returns are computed on inconsistent bases (arithmetic Growth-of-$1 via get_portfolio_price vs log returns via get_optimization_inputs/calculate_metrics) and inconsistent windows (full vs train vs test), so the same 'User Portfolio' can show subtly different return/Sharpe figures across the Portfolio, Optimization, Validation and Monte Carlo sections of the SAME combined report.

audits.purpose: The shared computational core of quant_reporter. metrics.py is the single-asset/portfolio performance-and-risk calculator (returns, vol, Sharpe/Sortino/Calmar, alpha/beta, drawdown, VaR/CVaR, distribution moments) returning formatted-string metrics + a plot_data dict. report_context.py is the ReportContext dataclass + build_context() factory that fetches data once and pre-computes the optimization inputs (mean returns, cov matrix, log returns, rfr) shared by every report generator. attribution.py implements standalone Brinson-Fachler sector attribution (allocation/selection/interaction effects).

metrics.py line 85 delegates to calculate_sortino_ratio(daily_returns, rfr) (metrics.py:11-22). Uses annualized arithmetic mean (mean*252) and downside std of negative returns *sqrt(252). NOTE: downside std uses .std() over only negative returns, not a target-relative semi-deviation.

report_context.py renders nothing - it is pure data assembly. build_context() returns a ReportContext dataclass holding formatted date strings, raw inputs, friendly-name mappings, the three price-data splits, and the train-period mean_returns/cov_matrix/log_returns + rfr consumed by every report module.

attribution.py renders nothing - compute_brinson_attribution() RETURNS a per-sector DataFrame (cols: Portfolio_Weight, Benchmark_Weight, Portfolio_Sector_Return, Benchmark_Sector_Return, Allocation_Effect, Selection_Effect, Interaction_Effect, Total_Effect + summary Portfolio_Return/Benchmark_Return/Excess_Return and a 'Total' row). Consumed/plotted by factor_report.py:224.

All three are headless compute/data layers; actual section and plot rendering happens in the *_report.py generators that import them (portfolio_report, optimization_report, validation_report, factor_report, monte_carlo_report).
concerns: DIVERGENCE-1 (annual return basis): ctx.mean_returns uses log-return*252 (opt_core.py:69) and drives all optimizers + get_portfolio_stats, while the performance table's Sharpe/Sortino use arithmetic simple-return*252 (metrics.py:13,84) and CAGR uses geometric (metrics.py:80). The Sharpe the optimizer maximizes and the Sharpe the report displays are not the same number.

DIVERGENCE-2 (growth/drawdown source): calculate_metrics is called on different upstream growth series - portfolio_report builds a Portfolio column then pct_changes it, while opt_core.get_portfolio_price (used by optimization/validation/factor reports at the call sites grep'd) produces a normalized-price weighted-sum series. metrics' drawdown/CAGR therefore depend on which growth construction the caller used. rebalancing.simulate_rebalanced_portfolio is a third, weight-drift construction.
DIVERGENCE-3 (VaR/CVaR meaning): metrics.calculate_var_cvar = 1-day historical; monte_carlo.calculate_simulation_metrics = full-horizon simulated. Both surface as 'VaR (95%)'/'CVaR (95%)'.
DIVERGENCE-4 (alpha/beta meaning): metrics CAPM single-factor alpha/beta vs factor_models FF3 alpha/betas, both labeled 'Alpha'.

RFR fallback mismatch: build_context else-branch defaults rfr=0.02 (report_context.py:90) but get_risk_free_rate's own failure fallback is 0.06 (opt_core.py:215); the effective default depends on which path triggers.
Annualization assumes daily everywhere in metrics.py/opt_core.py (252 hard-coded) with no frequency inference; only factor_models guards monthly. Non-daily input silently mis-annualizes.
CAGR years floor: metrics.py:78 years=max(total_days/365.25,1) caps the denominator at 1, overstating annualized return magnitude for sub-1-year windows and propagating into Calmar.

Sortino downside std (metrics.py:15-16) is std of negative returns only, not a target/MAR-relative downside deviation, and is divided into an arithmetic mean - inconsistent with the geometric/log bases used elsewhere.

metrics.calculate_metrics returns formatted STRINGS (e.g. '12.34%'), so any consumer needing the numeric value must re-parse; validation_report.py:32,42 comments confirm callers wrestle with the '(Asset)' suffix and string parsing - a recomputation/format hazard at the boundary.

research.family_notes: Design strategy: do NOT replace simulate_rebalanced_portfolio(price_data, weights_dict, rebalance_freq). Keep it as the zero-cost, single-target reference simulator. Extend it by (a) generalizing its single weights_dict into a *target-weight schedule* (a DataFrame of target weights indexed by rebalance date), and (b) factoring the cost/turnover/tax/tracking logic into standalone, independently-testable primitives that the engine calls. This keeps the institutional "no black box" promise: each number in a backtest report (turnover, cost drag, tax drag, tracking error) is produced by a primitive a user can call and unit-test in isolation. Recommended layering: target_weight_schedule (from optimizers/walk-forward) -> rebalance triggers (calendar/threshold) -> portfolio_turnover -> transaction_cost model -> simulate_strategy (the extended engine, nets costs, drifts weights, emits a trade blotter) -> realized_tracking_error / active_share / drawdown_stats on the output -> compare_strategies_oos + deflated/probabilistic Sharpe for honest OOS selection. Every primitive takes pandas/numpy and returns plain dicts/Series/DataFrames, matching the advanced_optimizers.py house style (np.ndarray weights, pd.DataFrame cov, validate-then-compute, logger.warning on edge cases). CRITICAL house-style note: the existing optimizers return np.ndarray weights in ticker order while simulate_rebalanced_portfolio takes a dict; the engine should accept BOTH (dict for one-shot, DataFrame schedule for dynamic) so it bridges optimizer output -> measured strategy without an adapter. Per the recommend=True philosophy, sensible default cost/triggers are exposed as explicit parameters, never hard-coded opinions; an optional recommend=True path can suggest a cost model from average daily volume but must be opt-in.

strategies.name: portfolio_turnover
strategies.summary: Compute one-way and two-way turnover between consecutive target/held weights — the atomic input every cost and tax model needs.
strategies.math_core: Pre-rebalance drifted weights w_i^- evolve from post-rebalance weights via w_i^- = w_i*(1+r_i)/sum_j w_j*(1+r_j). At a rebalance to target w_i^+: trade_i = w_i^+ - w_i^-. One-way turnover = 0.5*sum_i |trade_i| (fraction of portfolio traded); two-way = sum_i |trade_i|. Annualized turnover = period_turnover * (rebalances_per_year). Buys = sum(max(trade_i,0)), sells = sum(max(-trade_i,0)); for a self-financing rebalance buys≈sells.
strategies.key_params: weights_before (Series/array, drifted), weights_after (Series/array, target); convention='one_way' (default) | 'two_way'. No annualization inside the primitive — caller multiplies by frequency to keep it pure.
strategies.pitfalls: Comparing target-to-target instead of drifted-to-target massively overstates turnover (ignores that drift already moved you toward/away from target). Must use pre-rebalance DRIFTED weights, which is exactly what the existing loop computes at line 92-95 but discards. New-asset entry/full-exit are |w| not 2|w| in one-way. Cash/leverage: if weights don't sum to 1, normalize or the 0.5 factor is wrong.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def portfolio_turnover(weights_before, weights_after, convention='one_way') -> dict  # returns {'turnover': float, 'buys': float, 'sells': float, 'trades': pd.Series}
strategies.composes_with: Consumes the drifted current_weights the engine already tracks; feeds transaction_cost_model. Run on the weight_history_df rows that simulate_rebalanced_portfolio already returns to retrofit turnover onto existing backtests with zero engine changes.
references: Grinold & Kahn (2000), Active Portfolio Management, 2nd ed., ch. on transaction costs and turnover
DeMiguel, Garlappi & Uppal (2009), 'Optimal Versus Naive Diversification', RFS 22(5) — turnover as the key cost-sensitivity metric

strategies.name: transaction_cost_model
strategies.summary: Map a vector of trades into a cash cost using transparent commission + spread/slippage + (optional) square-root market-impact terms.
strategies.math_core: Per-rebalance cost C = sum_i [ commission_bps*|trade_i| + 0.5*spread_bps*|trade_i| + impact ]. Linear/proportional model: cost_frac = (commission_bps + 0.5*spread_bps)/1e4 * one_way_turnover (the standard 'cost = k * turnover' approximation). Almgren-Chriss-style nonlinear impact per name: impact_i = eta * sigma_i * (|trade_i|*V_port / ADV_i)^psi, with psi=0.5 the square-root law; eta≈1, sigma_i daily vol. Total drag subtracted from that period's gross return: r_net = r_gross - cost_frac.
strategies.key_params: trades (Series of weight deltas) or turnover (float); commission_bps=1.0, spread_bps=5.0, impact_model=None|'sqrt'|'linear', adv (Series, $ ADV per name, required for impact), portfolio_value=1.0, eta=1.0, psi=0.5, sigma (Series daily vol).
strategies.pitfalls: bps-on-notional vs bps-on-turnover confusion (factor-of-2 errors); double-counting half-spread (use 0.5*spread for a round-trip-per-side). Square-root impact needs ADV and breaks for thin names (impact can exceed 100% — clip and warn). Fixed per-trade minimums matter at small AUM and are NOT captured by a pure proportional model. Costs are path-dependent: applying a flat annual haircut understates the drag of high-turnover strategies. Keep impact opt-in (None default) so the canonical path stays a transparent linear model.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def transaction_cost_model(trades, commission_bps=1.0, spread_bps=5.0, impact_model=None, adv=None, sigma=None, portfolio_value=1.0, eta=1.0, psi=0.5) -> dict  # {'cost_frac': float, 'cost_cash': float, 'cost_breakdown': {'commission':..,'spread':..,'impact':..}}
strategies.composes_with: Takes portfolio_turnover output; its scalar cost_frac is subtracted inside simulate_strategy at each rebalance date. Pure function -> users can sweep bps assumptions to build a cost-sensitivity curve over any optimizer's weights.
references: Almgren & Chriss (2000), 'Optimal Execution of Portfolio Transactions', J. of Risk 3(2), 5-39
Almgren, Thum, Hauptmann & Li (2005), 'Direct Estimation of Equity Market Impact', Risk — empirical square-root law
Frazzini, Israel & Moskowitz (2018), 'Trading Costs', SSRN — real-world institutional cost magnitudes

strategies.name: generate_rebalance_dates
strategies.summary: Unify calendar-based and threshold/drift-band rebalancing into one trigger generator, generalizing the engine's current string/int freq logic.
strategies.math_core: Calendar: trigger on first trading day of each M/Q/Y or every N days (the existing rule). Threshold (band) rebalancing: at each step compute drift d_t = 0.5*sum_i |w_i^-(t) - w_i^target| (one-way distance to target); trigger a rebalance only when d_t >= tau (absolute band) OR per-asset |w_i^- - w_i^target| >= tau_i (Yamada/Masters tolerance bands). 'No-trade region': inside the band you let weights drift, cutting turnover/costs at the price of higher tracking error. Hybrid: calendar-checked but only execute if drift>tau.
strategies.key_params: index (DatetimeIndex), mode='calendar'|'threshold'|'hybrid', freq='M'|'Q'|'Y'|int, tau=0.05 (band width, fraction), per_asset_band=None, weight_history=None (required for threshold — drift must be measured against realized path).
strategies.pitfalls: Threshold mode is inherently path-dependent and cannot be precomputed from the index alone — it must run inside the simulation loop or be driven by an already-simulated weight path (chicken-and-egg). Too-tight a band = calendar-like cost with no benefit; too-wide = large tracking error and style drift. Look-ahead trap: deciding to rebalance using the close that you also trade at; use prior-close drift, trade at next open or apply slippage. Calendar 'first trading day' via month.diff() (the current trick) silently misfires on a leading partial month.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def generate_rebalance_dates(index, mode='calendar', freq='M', tau=0.05, per_asset_band=None, target_weights=None) -> pd.DatetimeIndex  # calendar precomputable; threshold returns a callable/predicate consumed by the engine
strategies.composes_with: Drop-in replacement for the inline rebalance_dates block (lines 44-68) in simulate_rebalanced_portfolio; the engine calls it instead of the hard-coded freq parsing. Calendar mode is fully back-compatible with the existing 'M'/'Q'/'Y'/int contract.
references: Donohue & Yip (2003), 'Optimal Portfolio Rebalancing with Transaction Costs', J. of Portfolio Mgmt 29(4)
Masters (2003) / Sun, Fan, Chen, Schouwenaars & Albota (2006), 'Optimal Rebalancing for Institutional Portfolios', JPM — tolerance bands vs calendar

strategies.name: simulate_strategy
strategies.summary: The extended engine: turns a target-weight SCHEDULE into a measured, cost-and-tax-aware strategy, emitting a trade blotter alongside the growth-of-$1 and weight history.
strategies.math_core: Generalizes the current loop. State: portfolio_value P, held weights w. Daily: w drifts by (1+r_i)/(1+w·r) and P*= (1+w·r) (unchanged). On a rebalance date d (from generate_rebalance_dates): target = schedule.loc[d]; trades = target - w^-; cost = transaction_cost_model(trades,...).cost_frac; apply w <- target and P <- P*(1-cost) (cost charged on rebalance). Optionally drag = cash_drag*r_cash on uninvested. Records each trade row -> blotter. Net CAGR/Sharpe/drawdown computed on the post-cost P. Reduces EXACTLY to simulate_rebalanced_portfolio when schedule is a single constant row and costs=0.
strategies.key_params: price_data (DataFrame), target_weights (dict for one-shot OR DataFrame schedule indexed by rebalance date), cost_model=None (dict of kwargs for transaction_cost_model), rebalance='M' | trigger spec, initial_value=1.0, cash_drag=0.0. Defaults reproduce the legacy zero-cost behavior.
strategies.pitfalls: Must preserve back-compat (return signature is a (Series, DataFrame) tuple today — add the blotter as a 3rd element or behind a flag to avoid breaking callers). Look-ahead if schedule rows are aligned to the close they're traded at — enforce that schedule weights are decided on data up to d-1. Charging cost on full target rather than on the trade overstates drag. Forgetting to renormalize after cost makes weights sum to <1 (becomes accidental leverage/cash). Survivorship/NaN prices mid-backtest silently zero a name's weight.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def simulate_strategy(price_data, target_weights, cost_model=None, rebalance='M', initial_value=1.0, cash_drag=0.0) -> dict  # {'wealth': pd.Series, 'weights': pd.DataFrame, 'blotter': pd.DataFrame, 'turnover': pd.Series, 'cost_drag': pd.Series, 'summary': dict}
strategies.composes_with: Top of the stack: consumes a target_weight_schedule built by repeatedly calling find_optimal_portfolio / optimize_risk_parity / optimize_hrp / Black-Litterman across walk-forward windows (run_rolling_windows already produces per-window weights). Its outputs feed metrics.calculate_metrics, realized_tracking_error, and compare_strategies_oos. Wraps, not replaces, simulate_rebalanced_portfolio.
references: Bailey & Lopez de Prado (2014), 'The Deflated Sharpe Ratio', JPM 40(5) — on honest backtest evaluation
Lopez de Prado (2018), Advances in Financial Machine Learning, ch. 13-14 (backtesting through cross-validation, the 7 sins of backtesting)

strategies.name: realized_tracking_error
strategies.summary: Ex-post tracking error, information ratio, and active share of a simulated strategy versus a benchmark — the standard 'how active was I, and was it worth it' panel.
strategies.math_core: Active return a_t = r_port,t - r_bench,t. Realized (ex-post) tracking error TE = std(a_t) * sqrt(252) (annualized sample std of active returns). Information Ratio IR = mean(a_t)*252 / TE. Active Share = 0.5*sum_i |w_i,port - w_i,bench| (Cremers-Petajisto), a holdings-based complement to return-based TE. Optionally ex-ante TE = sqrt((w_p - w_b)' Sigma (w_p - w_b)) for the forward-looking view using the existing cov_matrix.
strategies.key_params: portfolio_returns (Series), benchmark_returns (Series), periods_per_year=252; for active share: portfolio_weights (Series), benchmark_weights (Series); ex_ante_cov=None (DataFrame) to also return ex-ante TE.
strategies.pitfalls: Ex-post TE (realized) vs ex-ante TE (cov-based) routinely disagree — label which one. Sample TE is downward-biased on short windows and sensitive to outliers. IR is meaningless (sign-flips) when mean active return ≈ 0. Active share needs ALIGNED, same-universe weights (benchmark constituents reindexed onto the portfolio's union of tickers, missing=0) or it is overstated. Annualization factor must match the bar frequency.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def realized_tracking_error(portfolio_returns, benchmark_returns, portfolio_weights=None, benchmark_weights=None, ex_ante_cov=None, periods_per_year=252) -> dict  # {'tracking_error': float, 'information_ratio': float, 'active_share': float, 'ex_ante_te': float|None}
strategies.composes_with: Runs on simulate_strategy output (wealth->returns) against the benchmark already in ReportContext (friendly_benchmark). Reuses cov_matrix from get_optimization_inputs for ex-ante TE. Complements calculate_metrics (which already does alpha/beta/Sharpe) without duplicating it.
references: Roll (1992), 'A Mean/Variance Analysis of Tracking Error', JPM 18(4)
Cremers & Petajisto (2009), 'How Active Is Your Fund Manager? A New Measure That Predicts Performance', RFS 22(9) — Active Share

strategies.name: drawdown_stats
strategies.summary: Full drawdown profile — not just the single max number metrics already returns — including underwater duration, recovery time, and the worst-N drawdown table.
strategies.math_core: On wealth series W: peak_t = cummax(W); dd_t = W_t/peak_t - 1. Max drawdown = min(dd_t) (matches existing calculate_max_drawdown). Underwater periods = maximal runs where dd_t<0; for each: depth=min(dd), peak-to-trough duration, trough-to-recovery duration (until W reattains prior peak; ongoing if never). Calmar = CAGR/|maxDD| (already computed). Pain Index = mean(|dd_t|); Ulcer Index = sqrt(mean(dd_t^2)); Martin ratio = CAGR/Ulcer.
strategies.key_params: wealth (Series, growth-of-$1), top_n=5 (number of worst drawdowns to tabulate), periods_per_year=252.
strategies.pitfalls: Currently-ongoing drawdown has no recovery date — must report duration as right-censored, not drop it (a backtest ending in a drawdown is the dangerous case). Daily-mark drawdown understates intraday pain. Defining episodes by zero-crossings double-counts noisy oscillations near the peak — use a small epsilon. Single max-DD is a one-sample statistic with huge sampling error; the worst-N table is the honest view.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def drawdown_stats(wealth, top_n=5, periods_per_year=252) -> dict  # {'max_drawdown': float, 'underwater_curve': pd.Series, 'worst_drawdowns': pd.DataFrame[start,trough,recovery,depth,length], 'ulcer_index': float, 'pain_index': float}
strategies.composes_with: Pure post-processing of any wealth Series — simulate_strategy, simulate_rebalanced_portfolio, or a raw benchmark. Generalizes metrics.calculate_max_drawdown (which it can call) and the existing drawdown plot in opt_plotting.plot_drawdown_comparison feeds straight from underwater_curve.
references: Magdon-Ismail & Atiya (2004), 'Maximum Drawdown', Risk Magazine 17(10)
Martin & McCann (1989), 'The Investor's Guide to Fidelity Funds' — Ulcer Index

strategies.name: apply_tax_lots
strategies.summary: Tax-lot accounting layer that turns the engine's trade blotter into realized short/long-term capital gains, with FIFO/LIFO/HIFO lot selection and (optional) wash-sale flagging.
strategies.math_core: Maintain a per-asset FIFO/LIFO/HIFO queue of lots (qty, cost_basis, open_date). On a SELL of q shares: deplete lots per the chosen method; realized gain per depleted lot = (sale_price - basis)*depleted_qty; classify long-term if (sale_date - open_date) > 365 days else short-term. After-tax cash impact = -[ st_gain*st_rate + lt_gain*lt_rate ]. Tax drag_t subtracted (or netted at year-end). HIFO minimizes realized gain by always selling the highest-basis lot first. Wash sale: a loss is disallowed if the same/substantially-identical asset is bought within +/-30 days; deferral adds the disallowed loss to the replacement lot's basis.
strategies.key_params: blotter (DataFrame from simulate_strategy), prices (DataFrame), method='FIFO'|'LIFO'|'HIFO', st_rate=0.37, lt_rate=0.20, wash_sale=False, lt_threshold_days=365.
strategies.pitfalls: Fractional-share lot depletion and float rounding accumulate basis error over long backtests — track qty in shares, not weights. Wash-sale rules are jurisdiction-specific (US-centric here) and genuinely complex (substantially-identical, 61-day window) — ship a documented approximation and WARN rather than silently mis-state. Tax timing (annual settlement vs trade-time) changes after-tax CAGR materially. This is decision-support, NOT tax advice — must be labeled. Per the no-black-box rule, expose the full per-lot ledger, not just the aggregate drag.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def apply_tax_lots(blotter, prices, method='FIFO', st_rate=0.37, lt_rate=0.20, wash_sale=False) -> dict  # {'realized_gains': pd.DataFrame[date,asset,st_gain,lt_gain], 'tax_drag': pd.Series, 'lot_ledger': pd.DataFrame, 'after_tax_wealth': pd.Series}
strategies.composes_with: Strictly downstream of simulate_strategy — consumes its blotter to produce an after-tax wealth curve that can be re-run through drawdown_stats / calculate_metrics for a pre- vs post-tax comparison. Independent of the optimizers; works on any trade log.
references: Stein & Narasimhan (1999), 'Of Passive and Active Equity Portfolios in the Presence of Taxes', J. of Private Portfolio Mgmt
Berkin & Ye (2003), 'Tax Management, Loss Harvesting, and HIFO Accounting', Financial Analysts Journal 59(4)

strategies.name: compare_strategies_oos
strategies.summary: Out-of-sample horse-race that aligns multiple strategies' OOS returns and ranks them with overfitting-aware statistics, not just raw Sharpe.
strategies.math_core: Given OOS net-return series for K strategies, build a panel and report per-strategy CAGR/Sharpe/TE/maxDD plus pairwise significance. Probabilistic Sharpe Ratio PSR(SR*) = Phi( (SR_hat - SR*)*sqrt(n-1) / sqrt(1 - g3*SR_hat + ((g4-1)/4)*SR_hat^2) ), where g3=skew, g4=kurtosis — probability the true SR exceeds threshold SR*, correcting for sample length and non-normality. Deflated Sharpe Ratio = PSR evaluated at SR* = expected max of N independent trials' Sharpes (E[max] ≈ sqrt(Var_SR)*((1-γ)Z^-1[1-1/N] + γ*Z^-1[1-1/(N*e)])), deflating for the number of strategies tried. Ledoit-Wolf (2008) test gives p-values for Sharpe DIFFERENCES with HAC-robust, non-iid-aware standard errors.
strategies.key_params: returns_dict (dict[name->Series] of OOS net returns), benchmark_returns=None, n_trials=None (number of configs tried, for DSR — defaults to len(returns_dict)), sr_threshold=0.0, periods_per_year=252, alpha=0.05.
strategies.pitfalls: Picking the max-Sharpe strategy from many trials inflates the winner's Sharpe — DSR exists precisely to deflate this; reporting raw OOS Sharpe of the selected strategy is the single most common backtest sin. Strategies must be aligned on a COMMON OOS window and frequency or the comparison is invalid. Sharpe-difference tests assume enough independent observations; short OOS windows give no power. Overlapping walk-forward windows violate iid -> use HAC/Ledoit-Wolf SEs, not the naive t-test. n_trials must count EVERY configuration tried (including abandoned ones) to be honest — under-counting defeats the deflation.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def compare_strategies_oos(returns_dict, benchmark_returns=None, n_trials=None, sr_threshold=0.0, periods_per_year=252) -> dict  # {'summary': pd.DataFrame[CAGR,Sharpe,PSR,DSR,maxDD,TE], 'sharpe_diff_pvalues': pd.DataFrame, 'best_by_dsr': str}
strategies.composes_with: Caps the whole family: consumes OOS wealth/returns from multiple simulate_strategy runs (one per optimizer/cost assumption), themselves built on run_rolling_windows splits. The PSR/DSR sub-calculations are independently useful primitives (deflated_sharpe_ratio, probabilistic_sharpe_ratio) the report layer can surface. Directly upgrades the existing calculate_overfitting_score heuristic in validation_report.py with a statistically grounded version.
references: Bailey & Lopez de Prado (2014), 'The Deflated Sharpe Ratio: Correcting for Selection Bias, Backtest Overfitting and Non-Normality', JPM 40(5) — https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=2460551
Bailey & Lopez de Prado (2012), 'The Sharpe Ratio Efficient Frontier' (Probabilistic Sharpe Ratio), J. of Risk 15(2)
Ledoit & Wolf (2008), 'Robust Performance Hypothesis Testing with the Sharpe Ratio', J. of Empirical Finance 15(5)
White (2000), 'A Reality Check for Data Snooping', Econometrica 68(5)

research.family_notes:
These are overlays/sizers applied ON TOP of any base weight vector (from MVO, risk parity, HRP, Black-Litterman, etc.), so the natural design is a thin layer of pure functions that take base `weights` + a risk estimate (`cov`/`returns`) and return a leverage scalar, a scaled weight Series, or a glide-path of allocations. This keeps the "no black box" promise: every overlay is a one-screen function with a closed-form or LP/convex core, fully testable on synthetic inputs.

KEY CODEBASE CONVENTIONS I matched (from src/quant_reporter/): NumPy-style docstrings with Parameters/Returns/References; `cov_matrix` is a pd.DataFrame, `weights`/`mean_returns` are pd.Series or np.ndarray; annualization is returns*252, cov*252, vol*sqrt(252); reuse `opt_core.get_portfolio_stats`, `opt_core.validate_covariance_matrix`, `opt_core.regularize_covariance`, `metrics.calculate_max_drawdown`, `metrics.calculate_var_cvar`. Overlays should return plain dicts/Series like the Black-Litterman and rebalancing modules. Existing files: src/quant_reporter/advanced_optimizers.py (risk parity / HRP / min-corr / max-div), src/quant_reporter/opt_core.py (helpers + MVO), src/quant_reporter/metrics.py (drawdown, VaR/CVaR already present — CVaR optimizer extends this), src/quant_reporter/rebalancing.py (path simulator that CPPI/TIPP and vol-targeting glide-paths plug into).

DESIGN RECOMMENDATIONS: (1) Split each item into a stateless point-in-time sizer (e.g. `target_volatility_scalar`) AND a path/backtest wrapper that loops over a returns history — mirrors the existing `simulate_rebalanced_portfolio` split. (2) Vol-targeting, Kelly and risk-budgeting sizers should default to NO leverage cap surprises: expose `max_leverage` explicitly. (3) Per the library's opinions-behind-recommend rule, keep the multiplier/floor/target-vol/Kelly-fraction as plain required-or-defaulted params; only a higher-level `recommend=True` wrapper should pick "sensible" values (e.g. half-Kelly, 10% target vol, CPPI m=5). (4) Everything composes left-to-right: base optimizer -> (optional) risk-budget weights -> point-in-time leverage overlay -> rebalancing/path simulator -> metrics.

strategies.name: Volatility targeting (constant-vol scaling)
strategies.summary: Scale total exposure up/down each period so the portfolio's forecast annualized volatility matches a fixed target, leveraging in calm regimes and de-risking in turbulent ones.
strategies.math_core: Given base weights w0 and a forecast of portfolio vol sigma_hat (annualized), the leverage is L = target_vol / sigma_hat, clipped to [0, max_leverage]; scaled weights = L * w0 with (1 - L) (possibly negative) in cash. sigma_hat is typically an EWMA/rolling estimate: sigma_hat_daily^2 = (1-lambda) * r_t^2 + lambda * sigma_{t-1}^2, then annualize by sqrt(252). For a multi-asset book either (a) compute sigma_hat = sqrt(w0' Sigma w0) from a covariance forecast, or (b) use the realized vol of the base-portfolio return series. Volatility is far more forecastable/persistent than mean return, which is why scaling by 1/sigma_hat raises realized Sharpe for risk assets (leverage effect: returns don't rise to compensate higher vol).
strategies.key_params: target_vol=0.10 (annualized; 0.10-0.15 equities, lower for multi-asset); lookback/halflife: 20-60d realized or EWMA lambda~0.94 (RiskMetrics); max_leverage=1.5-2.0; min_leverage=0.0; rebalance band (e.g. only re-lever if |L_new/L_old - 1| > 0.1) to cut turnover.
strategies.pitfalls: Vol forecast lags fast crashes -> de-risks AFTER the drawdown (procyclical, can lock in losses on sharp V-shaped recoveries). Leverage amplifies losses and financing/borrow costs; small sigma_hat -> explosive leverage if not capped (divide-by-near-zero). Heavy turnover/transaction costs if no rebalance band. Targeting volatility is NOT targeting drawdown — fat tails still bite. Estimating sigma_hat in-sample then applying same-period is look-ahead; must use lagged estimate.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def target_volatility_scalar(forecast_vol: float, target_vol: float = 0.10, max_leverage: float = 1.5, min_leverage: float = 0.0) -> dict:  # returns {'leverage': L, 'forecast_vol': ..., 'target_vol': ..., 'capped': bool}. Companion: def forecast_portfolio_vol(weights, cov_matrix=None, returns=None, halflife: int = 60) -> float; and a path wrapper def apply_vol_target_path(returns: pd.Series, target_vol=0.10, halflife=60, max_leverage=1.5, rebalance_band=0.1) -> pd.DataFrame (columns: leverage, scaled_return, gross_return).
strategies.composes_with: Takes the output weights of ANY optimizer (MVO/min-var/risk-parity/HRP/BL) and the same cov_matrix used to build them (reuse opt_core.get_portfolio_stats / validate_covariance_matrix for sigma_hat). The scaled weights then feed rebalancing.simulate_rebalanced_portfolio and metrics.calculate_metrics. Stacks under Kelly/CPPI as an additional exposure multiplier.
references: Harvey, Hoyle, Korgaonkar, Rattray, Sargaison, van Hemert (2018), 'The Impact of Volatility Targeting', Journal of Portfolio Management
Moreira & Muir (2017), 'Volatility-Managed Portfolios', Journal of Finance
JP Morgan RiskMetrics Technical Document (1996) — EWMA vol, lambda=0.94

strategies.name: CVaR / Expected-Shortfall optimization (Rockafellar-Uryasev)
strategies.summary: Choose weights that minimize Conditional Value-at-Risk (expected loss in the worst (1-alpha) tail) via a linear program over historical/simulated scenarios — a coherent, convex tail-risk objective.
strategies.math_core: CVaR_alpha(w) = min over VaR-level zeta of [ zeta + 1/((1-alpha) T) * sum_t max(-r_t' w - zeta, 0) ]. Rockafellar-Uryasev (2000) linearize the hinge with auxiliary u_t >= 0: minimize_{w, zeta, u} zeta + 1/((1-alpha)T) * sum_t u_t s.t. u_t >= -r_t' w - zeta, u_t >= 0, sum w = 1, w in bounds (optionally w' mu >= R_min). This is a small LP (T+n+1 vars). At the optimum zeta* = VaR_alpha and the objective = CVaR_alpha. CVaR is coherent (sub-additive) so diversification never increases it, unlike VaR.
strategies.key_params: alpha=0.95 (tail beyond 95%; 0.975/0.99 for fatter-tail mandates); scenarios = empirical returns matrix (T x n) or Monte-Carlo-simulated paths (compose with existing monte_carlo); optional target_return R_min; weight bounds (0,1) long-only; long_only flag. Needs T >> n (e.g. T >= ~10n) for a stable tail.
strategies.pitfalls: Tail estimate is noisy with few scenarios — small T gives unstable, overfit corner solutions; bootstrap or simulate to densify the tail. Pure historical scenarios assume the past tail repeats. Requires an LP solver (scipy.optimize.linprog HiGHS is enough; cvxpy nicer but a heavier dep). Mixing daily scenarios with annual targets needs careful scaling. CVaR ignores the very extreme beyond what's sampled. Non-coherent if you accidentally optimize VaR instead.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def optimize_min_cvar(returns: pd.DataFrame, alpha: float = 0.95, target_return: float = None, bounds: tuple = (0, 1), long_only: bool = True) -> dict:  # returns {'weights': pd.Series, 'cvar': float, 'var': float, 'expected_return': float, 'success': bool}. Companion point estimator reusing metrics: def portfolio_cvar(weights, returns: pd.DataFrame, alpha=0.95) -> dict (wraps/vectorizes metrics.calculate_var_cvar on the portfolio return series).
strategies.composes_with: Drop-in alternative objective alongside opt_core MVO and advanced_optimizers — same weights-out interface, so it plugs into rebalancing and reporting unchanged. Scenarios can come straight from monte_carlo.simulate_portfolio_paths (per-asset) for a forward-looking tail. Reuses metrics.calculate_var_cvar for evaluation/back-checking the optimizer.
references: Rockafellar & Uryasev (2000), 'Optimization of Conditional Value-at-Risk', Journal of Risk 2(3)
Rockafellar & Uryasev (2002), 'Conditional Value-at-Risk for General Loss Distributions', Journal of Banking & Finance
Artzner, Delbaen, Eber, Heath (1999), 'Coherent Measures of Risk', Mathematical Finance

strategies.name: Generalized risk budgeting / Equal-Risk-Contribution (ERC)
strategies.summary: Solve for long-only weights whose percentage risk contributions match a chosen budget vector b (b = 1/n recovers risk parity); the general case allows arbitrary, intentional risk allocations across sleeves.
strategies.math_core: Marginal risk MRC_i = (Sigma w)_i / sqrt(w' Sigma w); total risk contribution RC_i = w_i * MRC_i, and sum_i RC_i = sigma_p (Euler). Risk budgeting solves RC_i / sigma_p = b_i for all i (b_i >= 0, sum b_i = 1). Two equivalent routes: (a) least-squares on contributions, minimize sum_i ( w_i (Sigma w)_i - b_i * w' Sigma w )^2; (b) the Maillard-Roncalli convex form, minimize_{y>0} (1/2) y' Sigma y - sum_i b_i ln(y_i), then renormalize w = y / sum(y) — this is strictly convex so the long-only solution is UNIQUE and solver-robust. ERC = special case b_i = 1/n.
strategies.key_params: risk_budget b: pd.Series summing to 1 (default equal = ERC); cov_matrix (annualized, regularized); solver tol/max_iter; long_only enforced by the log-barrier (y>0). No expected returns needed (pure risk allocation).
strategies.pitfalls: Degenerate/near-singular Sigma -> unstable contributions (regularize_covariance first). The naive least-squares objective (a) is non-convex with flat regions and can stall in SLSQP; prefer the convex log-barrier form (b). Risk contributions assume Gaussian/2nd-moment risk — understate tails. Long-only only; shorting breaks the convex form. Budgets are an explicit OPINION, so per the library's philosophy expose b as a required/defaulted param, not a hidden default.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def optimize_risk_budget(cov_matrix: pd.DataFrame, risk_budget: pd.Series = None, max_iter: int = 1000, tol: float = 1e-10) -> np.ndarray:  # risk_budget=None -> equal (ERC). Companion diagnostic: def risk_contributions(weights, cov_matrix: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.Series (percentage RC, sums to 1) — also useful for reporting on ANY weight vector.
strategies.composes_with: Generalizes the existing optimize_risk_parity (which is the b=1/n least-squares case) — could share opt_core.validate_covariance_matrix/regularize_covariance and be the engine risk_parity calls. risk_contributions(...) is a standalone overlay that annotates weights from MVO/HRP/BL for the optimization report (there's already plot_risk_contribution in opt_plotting). Output weights feed vol-targeting/Kelly overlays and the rebalancer.
references: Maillard, Roncalli, Teiletche (2010), 'The Properties of Equally Weighted Risk Contribution Portfolios', JPM
Roncalli (2013), 'Introduction to Risk Parity and Budgeting', Chapman & Hall/CRC
Bruder & Roncalli (2012), 'Managing Risk Exposures Using the Risk Budgeting Approach', SSRN

strategies.name: Kelly / fractional-Kelly sizing
strategies.summary: Set leverage on a base portfolio to maximize long-run geometric growth (full Kelly), then scale down by a fraction f for a far smoother equity curve at modest growth cost.
strategies.math_core: Continuous/Gaussian multi-asset growth-optimal weights: w* = Sigma^{-1} (mu - r_f 1), where mu is expected (arithmetic) returns and r_f the risk-free rate — proportional to inverse covariance times expected EXCESS return; this maximizes E[log(1+ r_p)]. Single-bet form f* = (p*b - q)/b = edge/odds. Fractional Kelly uses lambda * w* (0<lambda<=1). For a fixed base direction w0, the optimal Kelly leverage is L* = (mu_p - r_f) / sigma_p^2 (mean-variance growth max), applied as L = lambda * L*. Half-Kelly (lambda=0.5) keeps ~75% of the growth rate with ~half the variance/drawdown.
strategies.key_params: kelly_fraction lambda=0.5 (quarter-to-half is standard; full Kelly almost never used live); mu estimate (annualized, shrunk!); risk_free_rate (reuse opt_core.get_risk_free_rate, default 0.02-0.06); cov_matrix; max_leverage cap (Kelly can demand >1). For the full-vector form, optional long_only projection.
strategies.pitfalls: Brutally sensitive to mu estimation error — overstated mu -> overbetting -> ruinous drawdowns; the asymmetry (overbetting punished far worse than underbetting) is why practitioners use fractional Kelly + shrinkage/Bayesian mu. Sigma^{-1} unstable for ill-conditioned/large-n covariances (regularize or shrink). Assumes log-utility, continuous rebalancing, no transaction costs, stationary moments — all violated in practice. Unconstrained Kelly routinely implies large leverage; ALWAYS cap. Negative excess return -> wants to short/zero out.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def kelly_leverage(expected_return: float, variance: float, risk_free_rate: float = 0.02, kelly_fraction: float = 0.5, max_leverage: float = 1.5) -> dict:  # scalar version: {'leverage': L, 'full_kelly': L_star, 'fraction': lambda}. Full-vector version: def kelly_weights(mean_returns: pd.Series, cov_matrix: pd.DataFrame, risk_free_rate: float = 0.02, kelly_fraction: float = 0.5, max_leverage: float = 1.5, long_only: bool = False) -> pd.Series.
strategies.composes_with: kelly_leverage wraps any base portfolio: feed it opt_core.get_portfolio_stats(w0, mu, cov) outputs (return, vol) -> leverage multiplier, then scale w0 and run through the rebalancer (exactly like vol-targeting; both are exposure overlays and can be stacked or compared). kelly_weights is itself an optimizer producing a full weight vector, directly comparable to MVO (max-Sharpe is the tangency portfolio that Kelly leverages). Pair with metrics for drawdown checks of full vs fractional.
references: Kelly (1956), 'A New Interpretation of Information Rate', Bell System Technical Journal
Thorp (2006), 'The Kelly Criterion in Blackjack, Sports Betting, and the Stock Market', Handbook of Asset and Liability Management
MacLean, Thorp, Ziemba (2011), 'The Kelly Capital Growth Investment Criterion', World Scientific

strategies.name: Drawdown control via CPPI / TIPP
strategies.summary: Dynamically split capital between a risky base portfolio and cash to keep value above a protected floor: risky exposure = multiplier * (value - floor), so exposure shrinks toward zero as the cushion erodes.
strategies.math_core: Cushion C_t = V_t - F_t; risky exposure E_t = m * C_t, capped at [0, max_leverage * V_t]; cash = V_t - E_t. Multiplier m relates to the largest tolerated single-step crash: a drop of more than 1/m between rebalances breaches the floor (m=5 protects against ~20% gaps). CPPI: floor is fixed/grows at the cash rate, F_t = F_0 * exp(r_f t) (protection at a horizon). TIPP (Estep-Kritzman): floor RATCHETS up with the high-water mark, F_t = max(F_{t-1}, k * max_{s<=t} V_s) with protection level k (e.g. 0.8), locking in gains continuously. As C->0 the strategy monetizes into cash ('cash lock').
strategies.key_params: multiplier m=3-5; floor_pct (CPPI: e.g. 0.8 of initial, or PV of a future guarantee at r_f) / protection_level k (TIPP, e.g. 0.8 of high-water mark); rebalance frequency (daily/weekly — drives gap risk vs cost); max_leverage on the risky sleeve (often 1.0; >1 allowed); risk_free_rate for floor accretion.
strategies.pitfalls: GAP RISK: a market jump larger than 1/m between rebalances breaches the floor — discrete rebalancing means protection is not guaranteed (1987-style gaps). Higher m -> more upside but more gap risk. 'Cash lock' / monetization: after a drawdown the cushion collapses and the strategy sits in cash, missing the recovery (procyclical, like vol-targeting). Path-dependent and whipsaw-prone in choppy markets (buy-high/sell-low). TIPP's ratcheting floor is even more conservative -> lower long-run upside. Transaction costs accumulate with frequent rebalancing.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def cppi_exposure(portfolio_value: float, floor: float, multiplier: float = 5.0, max_leverage: float = 1.0) -> dict:  # one-step: {'risky_weight': e, 'cash_weight': 1-e, 'cushion': C}. Path/backtest wrapper: def simulate_cppi(risky_returns: pd.Series, multiplier=5.0, floor_pct=0.8, risk_free_rate=0.02, mode: str = 'CPPI', protection_level=0.8, rebalance_freq='M') -> pd.DataFrame (columns: value, floor, risky_weight, cushion) — mode in {'CPPI','TIPP'}.
strategies.composes_with: risky_returns is the base-portfolio return stream from ANY optimizer pushed through rebalancing.simulate_rebalanced_portfolio (pct-change of its growth-of-$1) — CPPI/TIPP wrap that single risky 'asset' vs cash. Output value path feeds metrics.calculate_metrics / calculate_max_drawdown to verify the floor held and quantify cash-lock drag. Naturally stacks with vol-targeting (vol-scale the risky sleeve, CPPI-protect the total).
references: Black & Jones (1987), 'Simplifying Portfolio Insurance', Journal of Portfolio Management
Perold & Sharpe (1988), 'Dynamic Strategies for Asset Allocation', Financial Analysts Journal
Estep & Kritzman (1988), 'TIPP: Time Invariant Portfolio Protection', Journal of Portfolio Management

strategies.name: Risk-parity / inverse-volatility leverage scaling (naive RP overlay)
strategies.summary: A lightweight, optimizer-free sizing rule: weight each asset by inverse volatility (1/sigma_i) for an equal-risk-ish base, optionally levered to a portfolio vol target — the cheap cousin of full ERC and the standard 'risk parity' leverage layer.
strategies.math_core: Inverse-vol weights w_i = (1/sigma_i) / sum_j (1/sigma_j), using sigma_i = sqrt(diag(Sigma)). This equalizes risk contributions exactly only when all pairwise correlations are equal; otherwise it's a fast approximation to ERC. The classic levered risk-parity book then applies a portfolio-level scalar L = target_vol / sqrt(w' Sigma w) (the vol-targeting overlay) so a low-vol bond-heavy sleeve is geared up to contribute equally — this is the bridge between inverse-vol weighting and leverage scaling.
strategies.key_params: cov_matrix (for diagonal vols); optional target_vol for the leverage step (default None = unlevered, fully-invested); max_leverage cap; uses only variances, ignores correlations (that's the point — robustness/transparency over precision).
strategies.pitfalls: Ignores correlations, so it equalizes risk only under the equicorrelation assumption — concentrates in low-vol assets that may be highly correlated (no real diversification). Levering low-vol assets imports rate/credit/liquidity tail risk (2022 was unkind to levered RP). Vol estimates are noisy at short windows. It is an APPROXIMATION to ERC; offer it as the transparent default and optimize_risk_budget as the exact version. Same procyclical leverage caveats as vol-targeting.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def inverse_volatility_weights(cov_matrix: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.Series:  # w_i proportional to 1/sigma_i, sums to 1. Optional convenience: def risk_parity_overlay(cov_matrix, target_vol: float = None, max_leverage: float = 2.0) -> dict (returns {'weights': pd.Series, 'leverage': L} — inverse-vol weights plus optional vol-target gearing).
strategies.composes_with: Pure-function base allocator usable standalone or as the warm-start initial_weights for advanced_optimizers.optimize_risk_parity / optimize_risk_budget (better SLSQP starting point than equal-weight). Its leverage step is literally target_volatility_scalar, so it demonstrates how two primitives compose. Output feeds the rebalancer and metrics like any weight vector.
references: Qian (2005), 'Risk Parity Portfolios: Efficient Portfolios Through True Diversification', PanAgora
Asness, Frazzini, Pedersen (2012), 'Leverage Aversion and Risk Parity', Financial Analysts Journal
Maillard, Roncalli, Teiletche (2010), JPM (inverse-vol as ERC under equicorrelation)

research.family: Tactical signal primitives (time-series momentum / trend, cross-sectional momentum & ranking, mean-reversion, carry)
research.family_notes:
Design fit: these are SIGNAL primitives, not auto-traders. Each returns a transparent, inspectable pandas object (a signal/score Series or DataFrame), never a trade. The user explicitly bridges signal -> allocation. Recommended composition pattern that already exists in this repo: (1) compute a cross-sectional SCORE Series with one of these primitives; (2) either map it to expected returns and feed `find_optimal_portfolio` / `optimize_*`, OR map it to Black-Litterman absolute/relative views via the existing `calculate_black_litterman_posterior(view_dict=..., relative_views=...)`, OR use it directly as a tilt that is then risk-normalized. Time-series signals (sign/strength of own trend, carry) naturally pair with a position-sizing primitive (volatility targeting per asset), and the resulting per-asset weights flow into `simulate_rebalanced_portfolio`. This keeps optimizers (risk model) and signals (return/tilt model) cleanly separated -- the institutional "alpha x risk" split.

Conventions matched from the codebase: snake_case; inputs are price/return wide DataFrames (dates x tickers, as produced by `get_data` -> `pct_change`/log) or `mean_returns`/`cov_matrix`; outputs are plain pandas Series/DataFrame/dict and np.ndarray; module-level `logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)`; nested helpers prefixed `_`; numpydoc docstrings with a References section; light input validation that raises ValueError; no look-ahead by construction (all rolling stats use only trailing windows, signals are lagged before they touch returns).

Two foundational utilities I reference repeatedly and recommend shipping FIRST because every other primitive composes with them:
  - `compute_trailing_volatility(returns, lookback=63, method='ewma', annualize=True) -> pd.DataFrame` (ex-ante per-asset vol; the MOP/Baltas-Kosowski vol estimate). EWMA (RiskMetrics, com chosen from lookback) or simple. Used for vol-scaling/risk-targeting and as the denominator in z-scores.
  - `volatility_target_positions(signal, returns, target_vol=0.10, vol_lookback=63, max_leverage=2.0, per_asset=True) -> pd.DataFrame` -- turns any signal (sign or strength) into vol-scaled per-asset positions w_{i,t} = signal_{i,t} * target_vol / sigma_hat_{i,t}, clipped to max_leverage. This is the single most important "signal -> position" bridge and is shared by TSMOM, trend rules, and carry. It is the institutional default for sizing trend/carry books.

Where I propose a primitive that overlaps an existing one, I say so (e.g. cross-sectional momentum SCORES can be consumed by all existing optimizers; OU/z-score reversion is a SHORT-horizon counterpart to the existing long-horizon allocators). I deliberately keep each primitive single-purpose and testable (deterministic given inputs, no network, no global state).

strategies.name: Time-series (12-1) momentum signal
strategies.summary: Per-asset trend signal from each asset's own trailing excess return; the canonical 'is this asset trending up or down?' building block for absolute (long/short) tilts.
strategies.math_core: For asset i at time t, signal raw_{i,t} = r_{i,t-k..t-1} = cumulative trailing return over a lookback of k periods, conventionally 12 months with a 1-period skip (12-1). Discrete sign rule: s_{i,t} = sign(raw_{i,t}) in {-1,0,+1} (Moskowitz-Ooi-Pedersen). Strength rule: s_{i,t} = raw_{i,t} / sigma_hat_{i,t} (trend t-stat-like), or clip(raw/scale, -1, 1). The position is then vol-scaled: w_{i,t} = s_{i,t} * target_vol / sigma_hat_{i,t}. sigma_hat is an ex-ante trailing (e.g. EWMA) vol using only data up to t-1 (no look-ahead). The optional 1-period skip avoids 1-month reversal contamination.
strategies.key_params: lookback (default 252 trading days ~12m); skip/gap (default 21 days ~1m, set 0 to disable); signal_type ('sign' default for robustness, or 'strength'); use_excess_return (default True, subtract risk-free); min_periods. For sizing: target_vol=0.10, vol_lookback=63, max_leverage=2.0.
strategies.pitfalls: Whipsaw / high turnover in choppy, range-bound markets (sign flips). Crash risk: trend books are short-gamma at sharp reversals (long after a rally, then the V-bottom hurts). Lookback overfitting -- prefer an ensemble of lookbacks (e.g. 1/3/12m) over a single 'optimal' one. Look-ahead leakage if sigma_hat or the signal is computed with same-period data; always lag the signal by >=1 period before applying to returns. Transaction costs and capacity are not modeled by the signal itself. Equities-long bias: on a long-only book a -1 signal can only mean 'flat', changing the strategy's character.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def time_series_momentum_signal(prices, lookback=252, skip=21, signal_type='sign', use_excess_return=True, risk_free_rate=0.0, min_periods=None) -> pd.DataFrame  # returns dates x tickers signal in [-1,1] (sign) or continuous (strength); already lagged-safe (uses only trailing data). Pair with volatility_target_positions(...) to get sized weights.
strategies.composes_with: Feed the sized positions into simulate_rebalanced_portfolio for a backtest. Or combine across assets and renormalize to use as the input weights to the existing rebalancer. The continuous strength version can be mapped to Black-Litterman absolute views (view_dict) -- e.g. scale signal to an expected-return view per asset and let calculate_black_litterman_posterior blend it with equilibrium. Risk sizing reuses the same cov/vol machinery as the optimizers. Orthogonal to (and combinable with) carry and cross-sectional momentum.
references: Moskowitz, Ooi & Pedersen (2012), 'Time Series Momentum', Journal of Financial Economics 104(2):228-250
Hurst, Ooi & Pedersen (2017), 'A Century of Evidence on Trend-Following Investing', AQR
Baltas & Kosowski (2013), 'Demystifying Time-Series Momentum Strategies', SSRN 2140091

strategies.name: Moving-average crossover trend signal
strategies.summary: Classic dual moving-average trend filter: long when fast MA is above slow MA, short/flat otherwise; a smooth, lower-turnover alternative to raw 12-1 sign.
strategies.math_core: Let MA_fast = SMA/EWMA of price over n_fast, MA_slow over n_slow (n_fast < n_slow). Discrete signal s_{i,t} = sign(MA_fast_{i,t} - MA_slow_{i,t}). Continuous/normalized signal (Baltas-Kosowski 'MAC'): x_{i,t} = (MA_fast - MA_slow) / (sigma_price * sqrt-type normalizer), optionally squashed by a response function (e.g. tanh or the Baltas-Kosowski phi(x)=x*exp(-x^2/4)/0.89 that fades very strong trends). Generalizes to a multi-pair ensemble (e.g. 8/24, 16/48, 32/96) averaged into one score (the canonical 'trend ensemble').
strategies.key_params: n_fast (default 50), n_slow (default 200) for the textbook 'golden/death cross'; ma_type ('sma' default or 'ewma'); signal_type ('sign' or 'normalized'); pairs (optional list of (fast,slow) tuples to ensemble, e.g. [(8,24),(16,48),(32,96)]). Sizing via volatility_target_positions.
strategies.pitfalls: Same whipsaw/turnover and short-gamma reversal risk as TSMOM, worse near the crossover where the signal dithers around zero. Strong autocorrelation between nearby (fast,slow) pairs -- an 'ensemble' of similar pairs gives false diversification. Lag: long MAs react slowly, so the signal can stay long well into a drawdown. SMA vs EWMA changes responsiveness materially; document the choice. Price-level (not return) based, so corporate actions / splits in raw prices distort it -- use adjusted prices (the repo's get_data already returns adjusted close).
strategies.proposed_primitive: def moving_average_crossover_signal(prices, n_fast=50, n_slow=200, ma_type='sma', signal_type='sign', pairs=None) -> pd.DataFrame  # dates x tickers; if pairs given, returns the averaged ensemble score. Combine with volatility_target_positions for sized weights.
strategies.composes_with: Interchangeable with time_series_momentum_signal anywhere a trend score is needed (same sizing + rebalancer pipeline). Can be averaged with the 12-1 signal to form a blended trend composite before sizing. Normalized output is BL-view-ready. Pure-pandas and deterministic -> trivially unit-testable (golden/death-cross on a synthetic ramp).
references: Baltas & Kosowski (2013), 'Demystifying Time-Series Momentum Strategies', SSRN 2140091 (MAC trading rule & response function)
Hurst, Ooi & Pedersen (2017), 'A Century of Evidence on Trend-Following Investing', AQR
Faber (2007), 'A Quantitative Approach to Tactical Asset Allocation', Journal of Wealth Management (10-month SMA timing)

strategies.name: Breakout (Donchian channel) trend signal
strategies.summary: Range-breakout trend rule: go long on a new n-day high, short/flat on a new n-day low; the basis of the classic Turtle/Donchian systems.
strategies.math_core: Upper channel U_{i,t}=max(price_{i,t-n..t-1}), lower channel L_{i,t}=min(price_{i,t-n..t-1}) (trailing, excluding t to avoid look-ahead). Entry signal: s_{i,t}=+1 if price_{i,t} >= U_{i,t}; s_{i,t}=-1 if price_{i,t} <= L_{i,t}; else hold previous state (state machine) or 0 (stateless). Normalized variant: position within range, x = 2*(price - midpoint)/(U - L) clipped to [-1,1]. Often combined with an ATR-based stop, but the signal primitive itself stays stop-agnostic.
strategies.key_params: lookback (default 55 for entry, 'Turtle' System 2; 20 is System 1); mode ('state' default = hold last breakout direction, or 'stateless'); signal_type ('sign' or 'range_position'); use_close (default True; can use intraday high/low if available).
strategies.pitfalls: False breakouts in mean-reverting regimes -> repeated small losses ('death by a thousand cuts'). Highly path/parameter sensitive (the chosen n). Discrete +1/-1 with a hold-state introduces hysteresis that unit tests must pin down. Gaps can trigger far through the channel (slippage not captured by the signal). With only daily adjusted close (repo default) you cannot model intrabar breakouts -- be explicit that this is a close-based approximation.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def breakout_signal(prices, lookback=55, mode='state', signal_type='sign') -> pd.DataFrame  # dates x tickers in {-1,0,+1} (or [-1,1] range position). Trailing windows only. Size with volatility_target_positions.
strategies.composes_with: Same signal->size->simulate_rebalanced_portfolio pipeline as the other trend rules; can be ensembled with MA-crossover and 12-1 into a single robust trend composite (institutions rarely run one rule). Stateless/range-position output can feed BL views or be used as a regime gate that scales a separate optimizer's weights up/down.
references: Faith (2007), 'Way of the Turtle' / Original Turtle Trading Rules (Donchian channel breakout)
Szakmary, Shen & Sharma (2010), 'Trend-following trading strategies in commodity futures', Journal of Banking & Finance 34(2):409-426
Clenow (2013), 'Following the Trend: Diversified Managed Futures Trading'

strategies.name: Cross-sectional momentum ranking signal
strategies.summary: Rank assets by trailing relative performance and tilt toward winners / away from losers; the workhorse 'relative-strength' score that drops straight into any existing optimizer.
strategies.math_core: For each date t, score raw_{i,t}=trailing return over [t-k, t-skip] (12-1 standard). Cross-sectional standardization at each t: z_{i,t}=(raw_{i,t}-mean_j raw_{j,t})/std_j raw_{j,t}, or rank-based r_{i,t}=2*(rank(raw_{i,t})/N) - 1 in [-1,1] (rank is more outlier-robust). Long-short construction: top-quantile minus bottom-quantile, dollar-neutral weights w_i proportional to z_i with sum(w)=0; long-only tilt: w_i proportional to max(z_i,0) renormalized to sum 1. The z-score vector is exactly the object an optimizer or BL view layer wants.
strategies.key_params: lookback (default 252), skip (default 21), method ('zscore' default or 'rank'), n_quantiles (default 5 for decile-style top/bottom; or quantile=0.2), neutral ('dollar' for L/S sum-0, or 'long_only'), winsorize (default 0.02 each tail).
strategies.pitfalls: Momentum CRASHES -- severe, forecastable left-tail losses in panic states / market rebounds (Daniel-Moskowitz 2016); a static rank book is short-gamma. Sector/factor concentration: raw momentum loads heavily on whatever rallied (often one sector) -- consider residual momentum or sector-neutralization. Crowding and high turnover at the rank boundaries. Small-N instability (with <~10 assets quantiles are noisy; standardize instead of bucketing). Survivorship/point-in-time universe matters for real use. The 1-month skip is important: without it you pick up short-term reversal.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def cross_sectional_momentum_score(prices, lookback=252, skip=21, method='zscore', winsorize=0.02) -> pd.DataFrame  # dates x tickers cross-sectionally standardized score (mean~0). Helper: def rank_to_long_short_weights(score_row, quantile=0.2, neutral='dollar') -> pd.Series.
strategies.composes_with: Strongest composition story in the repo. (1) Map latest score row to expected-return views: feed as BL view_dict / relative_views into calculate_black_litterman_posterior, then optimize -> a momentum-tilted, risk-aware portfolio. (2) Use score as the return vector mean_returns in find_optimal_portfolio (max-Sharpe / mean-variance) so the EXISTING optimizer sizes the momentum tilt under the covariance risk model. (3) Long-only tilt -> weights dict -> simulate_rebalanced_portfolio. Negatively correlated with value tilts (diversifies a value book; Asness et al.).
references: Jegadeesh & Titman (1993), 'Returns to Buying Winners and Selling Losers', Journal of Finance 48(1):65-91
Asness, Moskowitz & Pedersen (2013), 'Value and Momentum Everywhere', Journal of Finance 68(3):929-985
Daniel & Moskowitz (2016), 'Momentum Crashes', Journal of Financial Economics 122(2):221-247

strategies.name: Residual (factor-neutral) momentum score
strategies.summary: Rank assets on momentum of their factor RESIDUALS rather than raw returns, stripping out market/size/value beta to give a cleaner, less crash-prone momentum tilt.
strategies.math_core: Regress each asset's returns on factor returns over a rolling estimation window: r_{i,s} = alpha_i + B_i' F_s + eps_{i,s} (factors = MKT/SMB/HML or any supplied set). Take residuals eps_{i,s}, then the residual-momentum score = mean(eps over [t-k,t-skip]) / std(eps over the same window) (an information-ratio of residuals). Standardize cross-sectionally as in plain cross-sectional momentum. Because factor exposures are removed, the resulting long/short book has much smaller time-varying factor betas and ~2x the Sharpe of total-return momentum historically.
strategies.key_params: lookback (default 252), skip (default 21), estimation_window (default 252-756), factors (DataFrame of factor returns; defaults to the repo's Fama-French via fetch_fama_french_factors), standardize_by_resid_vol=True, min_obs.
strategies.pitfalls: Estimation error in betas (look-back and factor-set sensitive); rolling regressions are slower -- vectorize or cache. Requires aligned factor data (the repo already fetches FF; reuse it, mind timezone/calendar alignment). Residualizing can remove a real risk premium you wanted exposure to -- it is a deliberate neutralization, not free alpha. Still a momentum strategy: smaller but non-zero crash risk. Needs enough cross-section and history for stable regressions.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def residual_momentum_score(returns, factor_returns, lookback=252, skip=21, estimation_window=252, standardize=True) -> pd.DataFrame  # dates x tickers residual-momentum z-scores. Internally reuses run_factor_regression-style OLS (statsmodels) already in factor_models.
strategies.composes_with: Drop-in replacement for cross_sectional_momentum_score in every composition above (optimizer return vector, BL views, long-only tilt). Directly leverages existing factor_models (fetch_fama_french_factors, run_factor_regression). Natural partner to the existing Fama-French attribution report: show that the residual-momentum book has near-zero factor betas. Combine with value for a factor-balanced sleeve.
references: Blitz, Huij & Martens (2011), 'Residual Momentum', Journal of Empirical Finance 18(3):506-521
Gutierrez & Prinsky (2007), 'Momentum, reversal, and the trading behaviors of institutions', Journal of Financial Markets
Chaves (2016), 'Idiosyncratic momentum: U.S. and international evidence', Journal of Investing

strategies.name: Z-score mean-reversion signal
strategies.summary: Short-horizon contrarian signal: fade deviations of price (or a spread) from its rolling mean, sized by how many standard deviations away it is.
strategies.math_core: Given a series y (a single price, a log-price, or a constructed spread y = log P_a - beta*log P_b), rolling mean mu_{t}=mean(y_{t-w..t-1}), rolling sd sigma_{t}=std(y_{t-w..t-1}), z_{t}=(y_t-mu_t)/sigma_t. Contrarian signal s_{t} = -z_{t} (clipped to [-z_max, z_max] then scaled to [-1,1]) -- short when rich (z>0), long when cheap (z<0). Discrete band rule: enter when |z|>entry (e.g. 2), exit when |z|<exit (e.g. 0.5), with a state machine. For a pair, beta can be supplied or estimated by OLS / be the cointegration hedge ratio. Optionally gate on a stationarity test (ADF) computed separately.
strategies.key_params: window (default 20-60; this IS the reversion horizon), entry_z (default 2.0), exit_z (default 0.5), z_cap (default 3.0), mode ('continuous' = -z scaled, or 'bands' = discrete state), use_log_price=True. For pairs: hedge_ratio (float or 'ols').
strategies.pitfalls: Mean-reversion fails catastrophically when the series is actually TRENDING or regime-shifts (you keep adding to a loser) -- this is the mirror image of trend's whipsaw problem, and the two hedge each other. Non-stationary 'spreads' break (cointegration is not static; re-test). Rolling-window mean/sd are themselves moving targets; a structural break poisons mu and sigma. Short-vol / negatively-skewed payoff: many small wins, occasional large loss. Picking entry/exit bands is easy to overfit. Daily close-only data misses intraday reversion. NOT a buy-and-hold allocation tool -- it is explicitly short-horizon and turnover-heavy.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def zscore_reversion_signal(series, window=20, entry_z=2.0, exit_z=0.5, mode='continuous', z_cap=3.0) -> pd.DataFrame  # signal in [-1,1] (continuous) or {-1,0,+1} (bands). Companion: def build_spread(prices_a, prices_b, hedge_ratio='ols') -> pd.Series for pair construction.
strategies.composes_with: Sized with volatility_target_positions like the trend signals, then run through simulate_rebalanced_portfolio. Pairs naturally with the trend primitives as a regime hedge (trend + reversion is the classic CTA + stat-arb blend). Its z-score is also a generic 'rich/cheap' input that can scale any optimizer's weights (e.g. trim assets that are >2sigma rich). Pure-pandas, deterministic, easy to unit-test on a sine wave.
references: Avellaneda & Lee (2010), 'Statistical Arbitrage in the U.S. Equities Market', Quantitative Finance 10(7):761-782 (s-score)
Gatev, Goetzmann & Rouwenhorst (2006), 'Pairs Trading: Performance of a Relative-Value Arbitrage Rule', Review of Financial Studies 19(3):797-827
Chan (2013), 'Algorithmic Trading: Winning Strategies and Their Rationale'

strategies.name: Ornstein-Uhlenbeck half-life & mean-reversion parameters
strategies.summary: Fit a continuous-time OU process to a series/spread to get a model-based reversion speed, equilibrium mean, vol, and half-life -- so reversion windows are estimated, not guessed.
strategies.math_core: OU SDE: dy_t = theta*(mu - y_t) dt + sigma dW_t. Discretize as AR(1): y_t = a + b*y_{t-1} + eps_t, estimated by OLS. Map back: theta = -ln(b)/dt (mean-reversion speed; requires 0<b<1), mu = a/(1-b) (long-run mean), sigma_eq = std(eps)*sqrt(-2*ln(b)/((1-b^2)*dt)). Half-life H = ln(2)/theta (expected time to revert halfway). Equilibrium std = sigma / sqrt(2*theta). These parameters directly set a principled z-score window (~ a small multiple of the half-life) for the reversion signal above.
strategies.key_params: dt (default 1.0, in the series' own time units), price_transform ('log' default or 'level'), min_obs (default 60). Returns a dict, e.g. {'theta':..., 'mu':..., 'sigma':..., 'half_life':..., 'equilibrium_std':..., 'ar1_beta':..., 'is_mean_reverting': bool}.
strategies.pitfalls: If b>=1 (or <=0) the series is NOT mean-reverting and half-life is undefined/negative -- must guard and flag (don't return garbage). OU assumes a constant mu/theta over the window; real spreads drift and break (combine with a rolling refit and a stationarity test). Discretization/estimation bias for fast reversion or short samples. Calibrating on a non-stationary series gives confidently-wrong parameters -- this is the most common failure (see Hudson & Thames 'caveats'). It estimates parameters only; it does not itself decide trades.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def fit_ornstein_uhlenbeck(series, dt=1.0, price_transform='log', min_obs=60) -> dict  # OLS/AR(1) calibration -> {theta, mu, sigma, half_life, equilibrium_std, ar1_beta, is_mean_reverting}.
strategies.composes_with: Feeds zscore_reversion_signal: use half_life to set window (e.g. window = round(k * half_life)) and equilibrium_std to set the bands -- turning the heuristic reversion signal into a model-grounded one. Pairs with build_spread for cointegrated pairs. Outputs a plain dict (matches repo style, e.g. risk-contribution metadata). Deterministic given the series -> unit-test against a simulated OU path with known theta.
references: Avellaneda & Lee (2010), 'Statistical Arbitrage in the U.S. Equities Market', Quantitative Finance 10(7):761-782
Leung & Li (2015), 'Optimal Mean Reversion Trading with Transaction Costs and Stop-Loss Exit', International Journal of Theoretical and Applied Finance
Hudson & Thames, 'Caveats in Calibrating the OU Process' (https://hudsonthames.org/caveats-in-calibrating-the-ou-process/)

strategies.name: Carry signal (model-free expected-return-if-prices-unchanged)
strategies.summary: Rank/size assets by their carry -- the return earned if prices stay flat (yield, roll, dividend) -- an ex-ante, model-free expected-return proxy that complements momentum and value.
strategies.math_core: Carry_{i,t} = expected return assuming price unchanged. Implementations by asset class: equities -> dividend yield (optionally + buyback yield); bonds -> yield-to-maturity (or yield minus short rate = term/roll carry); futures -> annualized roll yield (F_near - F_far)/F_far / dt, or (Spot - F)/F; FX -> interest-rate differential (forward points). Cross-sectional use: standardize carry_{i,t} into a z-score / rank exactly like cross-sectional momentum and go long high-carry, short low-carry. Time-series use: scale a per-asset position by its own carry sign/strength, then vol-target. Carry is a *characteristic input*; the primitive standardizes/aligns whatever raw carry the user supplies rather than fetching exotic data.
strategies.key_params: carry_source (user-supplied DataFrame of raw carry, e.g. yields/roll yields, dates x tickers), method ('zscore' default or 'rank'), annualize (default True), neutral ('dollar' or 'long_only'), winsorize (default 0.02). Convenience: def dividend_carry(prices, dividends) -> pd.DataFrame and def roll_yield_carry(front, back, days_between) -> pd.DataFrame.
strategies.pitfalls: Carry's classic failure mode is the 'carry crash' / picking up pennies in front of a steamroller -- high-carry assets (e.g. high-yield FX) crash together in risk-off, with strong negative skew (Koijen et al.; Brunnermeier et al. on FX carry). Carry is a noisy expected-return proxy, not realized return. Data quality: yields/roll yields must be point-in-time and currency/units-consistent (a frequent bug). Pure carry is highly exposed to a single risk factor (e.g. funding/liquidity) -- diversify with momentum (carry and momentum are weakly/negatively correlated). For equities, dividend yield alone misses buybacks and confounds with the value factor.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def carry_score(carry_source, method='zscore', annualize=True, winsorize=0.02) -> pd.DataFrame  # standardizes a user-supplied raw-carry DataFrame into a cross-sectional score (dates x tickers). Helpers: dividend_carry(...), roll_yield_carry(...).
strategies.composes_with: Identical composition surface to cross_sectional_momentum_score: use as expected-return views in calculate_black_litterman_posterior, as the mean_returns vector for find_optimal_portfolio, or as a long-only tilt into simulate_rebalanced_portfolio. The canonical institutional sleeve combines carry + momentum + value scores (z-score average) before sizing -- all three are interchangeable Series in this design, so the user just averages them. Vol-target the per-asset version for a time-series carry book.
references: Koijen, Moskowitz, Pedersen & Vrugt (2018), 'Carry', Journal of Financial Economics 127(2):197-225
Brunnermeier, Nagel & Pedersen (2008), 'Carry Trades and Currency Crashes', NBER Macroeconomics Annual
Asness, Moskowitz & Pedersen (2013), 'Value and Momentum Everywhere', Journal of Finance 68(3):929-985 (combining carry-like and momentum signals)

strategies.name: Volatility-target position sizing (signal -> weights bridge)
strategies.summary: Foundational utility that converts any signal (sign or strength) into per-asset positions scaled to a target volatility, with a leverage cap -- the standard institutional bridge from alpha signal to tradable weights.
strategies.math_core: w_{i,t} = signal_{i,t} * target_vol / sigma_hat_{i,t}, where sigma_hat_{i,t} is ex-ante trailing per-asset vol (EWMA/simple) using only data up to t-1. Clip total or per-asset leverage to max_leverage. Optional portfolio-level overlay: scale the whole book by target_vol / sigma_hat_portfolio so realized portfolio vol tracks the target (Moskowitz-Ooi-Pedersen scale each position to constant vol; Baltas-Kosowski add a correlation/vol-parity adjustment). Constant-vol scaling also dampens momentum crashes (Daniel-Moskowitz, Harvey et al.).
strategies.key_params: target_vol (default 0.10 annualized), vol_lookback (default 63 days ~3m), vol_method ('ewma' default or 'simple'), max_leverage (default 2.0), scaling ('per_asset' default, or 'portfolio' to also hit a book-level target using cov), min_periods.
strategies.pitfalls: Vol estimate is itself laggy -- it underestimates risk right before vol spikes (the moment it matters most). Inverse-vol sizing piles INTO low-vol assets, which can quietly concentrate risk or create crowding. Leverage cap interacts non-trivially with the signal (a capped book is no longer 'constant vol'). Portfolio-level scaling needs a covariance matrix -> reuse validate_covariance_matrix/regularize_covariance to avoid blow-ups from a near-singular cov. Must lag sigma_hat to avoid look-ahead. Realized vol will still overshoot target in fat-tailed regimes.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def volatility_target_positions(signal, returns, target_vol=0.10, vol_lookback=63, vol_method='ewma', max_leverage=2.0, scaling='per_asset', cov=None) -> pd.DataFrame  # dates x tickers sized positions. Companion: def compute_trailing_volatility(returns, lookback=63, method='ewma', annualize=True) -> pd.DataFrame.
strategies.composes_with: The shared sizing layer for EVERY time-series signal here (TSMOM, MA-crossover, breakout, z-score reversion, time-series carry). When scaling='portfolio', it consumes a cov_matrix and reuses the repo's covariance validation/regularization, making it the clean handoff point between the SIGNAL primitives and the existing RISK/optimizer primitives. Output positions flow straight into simulate_rebalanced_portfolio. Single-purpose and deterministic -> straightforward property tests (realized vol ~ target on synthetic data; leverage never exceeds cap).
references: Moskowitz, Ooi & Pedersen (2012), 'Time Series Momentum', Journal of Financial Economics 104(2):228-250 (ex-ante vol scaling)
Baltas & Kosowski (2013), 'Demystifying Time-Series Momentum Strategies', SSRN 2140091 (vol & correlation parity)
Harvey, Hoyle, Korgaonkar, Rattray, Sargaison & van Hemert (2018), 'The Impact of Volatility Targeting', Journal of Portfolio Management

research.family_notes: Two sub-families that are most useful TOGETHER: (A) robust estimators that harden the (mean_returns, cov_matrix) inputs every existing optimizer already consumes, and (B) factor-tilt portfolio builders layered on the existing Fama-French engine. Codebase conventions I matched (verified by reading src/quant_reporter/): covariance is a pd.DataFrame with index==columns==tickers and is ANNUALIZED (x252); mean returns are an annualized pd.Series; weights are np.ndarray in advanced_optimizers.py but dict in rebalancing/reports; validation goes through opt_core.validate_covariance_matrix / regularize_covariance; the existing get_optimization_inputs() returns (mean_returns, cov_matrix, log_returns) and there is already a denoise_covariance_matrix() (eigenvalue clipping) and a Bayesian shrinkage of means inside black_litterman.py. scikit-learn>=1.0 is ALREADY a hard dependency (sklearn.covariance.LedoitWolf/OAS/ledoit_wolf are importable), as are scipy and statsmodels -- so no new deps are needed. Design intent per the library philosophy: every estimator below is a pure, transparent drop-in that returns the SAME shape the existing optimizers expect, so robust covariance/mean simply slots in front of find_optimal_portfolio, optimize_risk_parity, optimize_max_diversification, calculate_black_litterman_posterior, etc. All opinions (e.g. which shrinkage target, default tilt strength) stay as explicit params; recommendation logic belongs behind the separate recommend=True flag, not inside these primitives. KEY CROSS-CUTTING PITFALL: get_optimization_inputs already multiplies cov by 252; sklearn estimators must be fit on the SAME PERIODICITY returns and then annualized by the SAME factor, or scale will silently double-count. Several primitives below take returns and return annualized objects to keep that contract in one place.

strategies.name: Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage covariance
strategies.summary: Shrink the noisy sample covariance toward a structured target (constant-correlation or single-index) by an analytically optimal intensity, giving a well-conditioned, invertible matrix that dramatically stabilizes any downstream optimizer.
strategies.math_core: Estimator: Sigma_hat = delta * F + (1 - delta) * S, where S is the sample covariance, F is the shrinkage target (Ledoit-Wolf 2004 uses the constant-correlation target: keep sample variances on the diagonal, replace every pairwise correlation by the average sample correlation r_bar; the 2003 single-index target uses an implied CAPM/market structure). The optimal intensity delta* = kappa/T minimizes E||Sigma_hat - Sigma||_F^2 in Frobenius norm, with kappa = (pi_hat - rho_hat)/gamma_hat: pi_hat = sum of asymptotic variances of the sample covariance entries, rho_hat = covariance between sample-cov entries and the target, gamma_hat = ||S - F||_F^2 (mis-specification of the target). delta is clipped to [0,1]. Result is guaranteed positive-definite for delta>0 even when T < N. (Oracle Approximating Shrinkage, OAS, is the same shrink-to-scaled-identity idea with a different intensity formula and often shrinks harder for small T.)
strategies.key_params: returns: pd.DataFrame (single-periodicity, e.g. daily log returns from get_optimization_inputs's log_returns); target='constant_correlation' (default) | 'single_index' | 'identity'; periods_per_year=252 (annualization factor to MATCH get_optimization_inputs); delta=None (None => compute optimal; or pin a manual override in [0,1]). Defaults chosen so the call is a pure drop-in for the existing annualized cov.
strategies.pitfalls: (1) Annualization double-count: the existing get_optimization_inputs already x252; fit shrinkage on the same returns and annualize with the SAME factor exactly once.
