(2) sklearn.covariance.ledoit_wolf assumes rows=samples, cols=features and uses a shrink-to-identity target -- that is NOT the constant-correlation target of the 2004 paper and is scale-sensitive, so standardize or implement the constant-correlation target explicitly for the canonical behavior. (3) Shrinkage biases variances/correlations toward the target; report the realized delta so users see how much structure was imposed (delta near 1 means 'almost all target'). (4) Do not shrink an already-denoised matrix without thinking -- composing denoise_covariance_matrix then shrinkage applies two regularizers.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def ledoit_wolf_covariance(returns: pd.DataFrame, target: str = 'constant_correlation', periods_per_year: int = 252, delta: float | None = None) -> dict:  # returns {'cov_matrix': pd.DataFrame (annualized, PSD), 'shrinkage': float (delta* used), 'target': str, 'sample_cov': pd.DataFrame, 'target_matrix': pd.DataFrame}
strategies.composes_with: Drop-in replacement for the cov_matrix produced by opt_core.get_optimization_inputs. Feeds DIRECTLY into find_optimal_portfolio, objective_min_variance/objective_neg_sharpe, optimize_risk_parity, optimize_min_correlation, optimize_max_diversification, optimize_hrp, and as Sigma into calculate_black_litterman_posterior / calculate_implied_equilibrium_returns. Passes validate_covariance_matrix by construction (PD), so it removes most regularize_covariance fallbacks. Pairs naturally with james_stein_mean below to harden BOTH optimizer inputs.
references: Ledoit & Wolf (2004), 'Honey, I Shrunk the Sample Covariance Matrix', J. Portfolio Management 30(4):110-119 (http://www.ledoit.net/honey.pdf)
Ledoit & Wolf (2003), 'Improved Estimation of the Covariance Matrix of Stock Returns with an Application to Portfolio Selection', J. Empirical Finance
Chen, Wiesel, Eldar & Hero (2010), 'Shrinkage Algorithms for MMSE Covariance Estimation' (OAS), IEEE Trans. Signal Processing

strategies.name: James-Stein / Bayes-Stein mean shrinkage
strategies.summary: Shrink each asset's noisy sample mean toward a common grand mean (or the minimum-variance-portfolio return) by a data-driven intensity, fixing the single biggest failure mode of max-Sharpe optimization: garbage expected-return inputs.
strategies.math_core: James-Stein: mu_hat = (1 - w) * mu_sample + w * mu_target * 1, with target the grand mean mu_target = (1'Sigma^{-1} mu) / (1'Sigma^{-1} 1) (the GMV-implied mean) or a simple cross-sectional average. Shrinkage weight w = min(1, ( (N-2)/T ) / ( (mu_sample - mu_target*1)' Sigma^{-1} (mu_sample - mu_target*1) )) -- larger when assets are few/noisy (small T) and when sample means are close to the target. Jorion's (1986) Bayes-Stein uses w_BS = (N+2) / ( (N+2) + T * (mu_sample - mu_target)' Sigma^{-1} (mu_sample - mu_target) ) and also inflates the posterior covariance by a term reflecting estimation uncertainty in the mean. Both are 'admissible' improvements: classical sample mean is inadmissible for N>=3 (Stein's paradox).
strategies.key_params: mean_returns: pd.Series (annualized, from get_optimization_inputs); cov_matrix: pd.DataFrame (annualized; ideally the Ledoit-Wolf one); n_obs: int (T, the number of return observations used to estimate the mean -- REQUIRED, drives the intensity); target='grand_mean' (default) | 'gmv' | float; method='james_stein' (default) | 'bayes_stein'. Returns shrunk means plus the realized weight so the tilt is transparent.
strategies.pitfalls: (1) Forgetting n_obs / passing annualized-but-wrong T makes w meaningless -- T must be the actual sample size behind the mean, not 252. (2) Shrinking toward the grand mean compresses cross-sectional dispersion, which can NEUTER a deliberate factor tilt -- if the user wants a tilt, shrink less or skip means entirely (min-variance/risk-parity ignore means anyway). (3) Bayes-Stein's covariance inflation must NOT be double-applied if you also use Black-Litterman's posterior covariance. (4) Negative or near-zero quadratic form can blow up w; clip w to [0,1].
strategies.proposed_primitive: def james_stein_mean(mean_returns: pd.Series, cov_matrix: pd.DataFrame, n_obs: int, target: str = 'grand_mean', method: str = 'james_stein') -> dict:  # returns {'mean_returns': pd.Series (shrunk), 'shrinkage': float (w), 'target_return': float, 'cov_adjustment': pd.DataFrame | None}
strategies.composes_with: Drop-in replacement for the mean_returns fed to find_optimal_portfolio(objective_neg_sharpe, ...) and get_portfolio_stats. Most valuable stacked AFTER ledoit_wolf_covariance (shrink both inputs) and BEFORE the max-Sharpe optimizer. Conceptually adjacent to calculate_black_litterman_posterior (both are shrinkage-of-means); offer it as the lightweight, view-free alternative to BL when the user has no views. Ignored cleanly by mean-agnostic optimizers (risk parity, HRP, max-diversification).
references: Jorion (1986), 'Bayes-Stein Estimation for Portfolio Analysis', J. Financial and Quantitative Analysis 21(3):279-292
James & Stein (1961), 'Estimation with Quadratic Loss', Proc. 4th Berkeley Symposium
Kan & Zhou (2007), 'Optimal Portfolio Choice with Parameter Uncertainty', JFQA 42(3):621-656

strategies.name: Resampled (Michaud) efficient portfolios
strategies.summary: Monte-Carlo the estimation error in (mu, Sigma), re-optimize on each draw, and average the resulting weights -- yielding stable, diversified portfolios that don't lurch on tiny input changes and don't pile into a few corner assets.
strategies.math_core: For b = 1..B: draw a resampled return panel of length T from N(mu_hat, Sigma_hat) (or block-bootstrap historical returns), recompute (mu_b, Sigma_b), solve the SAME constrained optimizer for a target (max-Sharpe, min-variance, or a rank along the frontier) to get w_b. The resampled portfolio is w_bar = (1/B) sum_b w_b (optionally averaged at matched frontier RANK to build a resampled frontier). Averaging is a shrinkage in weight space: because the optimizer is a nonlinear, kinked map of noisy inputs, E[w(mu_b,Sigma_b)] != w(mu_hat,Sigma_hat), and the average is far less extreme and obeys all linear constraints (a convex combination of feasible w_b is feasible). Report cross-draw weight dispersion as an uncertainty band.
strategies.key_params: returns: pd.DataFrame; optimizer: Callable (e.g. a closure over find_optimal_portfolio with chosen objective + bounds + constraints) OR objective='max_sharpe'|'min_variance'; n_samples=500 (B; convergence ~ few hundred); block_size=None (None => parametric Normal resample; int => block bootstrap to preserve autocorrelation/fat tails); periods_per_year=252; seed=None. Returns averaged weights + per-asset dispersion.
strategies.pitfalls: (1) Parametric Normal resampling inherits Gaussian thin tails -- prefer block bootstrap for realistic fat tails/serial correlation. (2) Statistically it 're-uses' the same point estimate as truth, so it reduces VARIANCE of the allocation but not BIAS in mu_hat (Scherer's critique) -- compose with shrinkage estimators, don't treat resampling as a substitute for them. (3) Long-only constraints make w_bar inherit an upward bias for high-vol assets near 0 bounds; document it. (4) B*optimizer cost is the runtime; reuse the existing SLSQP path and parallelize if needed. (5) Not invariant to constraint formulation -- the averaged frontier is path/rank-definition dependent.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def resample_portfolio(returns: pd.DataFrame, objective: str = 'max_sharpe', n_samples: int = 500, block_size: int | None = None, bounds=(0, 1), constraints=None, periods_per_year: int = 252, seed: int | None = None) -> dict:  # returns {'weights': pd.Series (averaged), 'weight_std': pd.Series, 'all_weights': np.ndarray (B x N), 'n_samples': int}
strategies.composes_with: Wraps, rather than replaces, the existing engine: each inner solve calls find_optimal_portfolio with objective_neg_sharpe/objective_min_variance and build_constraints, so sector caps/mins flow through unchanged. Inner (mu,Sigma) per draw can themselves be hardened with ledoit_wolf_covariance + james_stein_mean (the recommended stack). Output weights.to_dict() feeds simulate_rebalanced_portfolio for backtesting, and the all_weights matrix plugs into the existing Monte Carlo / validation reporting for uncertainty bands. Directly comparable on the same efficient-frontier plot already produced by calculate_efficient_frontier_curve.
references: Michaud & Michaud (1998, 2nd ed. 2008), 'Efficient Asset Management', and Michaud & Michaud (2008) 'Estimation Error and Portfolio Optimization: A Resampling Solution', J. Investment Management (https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=2658657)
Scherer (2002), 'Portfolio Resampling: Review and Critique', Financial Analysts Journal 58(6):98-109

strategies.name: Characteristic z-score tilt portfolio
strategies.summary: Turn a raw stock characteristic (value, quality, size, momentum, low-vol) into long-only or long-short weights via cross-sectional standardization, optionally tilting a base/benchmark weight rather than starting from cash.
strategies.math_core: Cross-sectionally standardize the characteristic at each rebalance: z_i = (c_i - mean(c)) / std(c), optionally winsorized at +/-3 and sign-flipped so 'good' is positive (e.g. low B/M => negative value z, so use -z for a value tilt; for low-vol, z on -volatility). Long-short (dollar-neutral, characteristic = 'parametric portfolio policy' tilt): w_i = w_base_i + (kappa/N) * z_i, so sum(tilt)=0 and the active book has unit-ish exposure scaled by kappa (Brandt-Santa-Clara-Valkanov 'parametric portfolio policies'). Long-only score-tilt: w_i proportional to max(w_base_i * exp(kappa * z_i), 0) (or a linear w_base_i*(1+kappa*z_i) clipped at 0), renormalized to sum 1. kappa controls tilt aggressiveness; z>0 overweights, z<0 underweights. Combine multiple characteristics by averaging their z-scores (composite score) BEFORE tilting.
strategies.key_params: characteristic: pd.Series (one value per ticker, current cross-section); base_weights: pd.Series | None (None => equal or market-cap base); tilt_strength=1.0 (kappa); mode='long_short' (dollar-neutral) | 'long_only'; winsorize=3.0 (std clips, None to disable); higher_is_better=True (sign of the tilt). Returns weights that sum to 0 (long_short) or 1 (long_only) plus the z-scores for transparency.
strategies.pitfalls: (1) Look-ahead/point-in-time: characteristics must be known at rebalance (lag fundamentals); this primitive only transforms a cross-section, so the CALLER owns timing. (2) Sector/size concentration: a naive value tilt loads up cheap sectors -- compose with build_constraints sector caps or neutralize the characteristic within sectors first. (3) Outliers dominate without winsorization; a single extreme z hijacks the book. (4) Long-only exp-tilt is not scale-free -- large kappa pushes to corner weights; clip/renormalize and cap per-name weight. (5) Dollar-neutral != beta-neutral: the long-short book can still carry market beta unless beta-hedged (see factor_neutralize_returns).
strategies.proposed_primitive: def characteristic_tilt_weights(characteristic: pd.Series, base_weights: pd.Series | None = None, tilt_strength: float = 1.0, mode: str = 'long_short', winsorize: float | None = 3.0, higher_is_better: bool = True) -> dict:  # returns {'weights': pd.Series, 'z_scores': pd.Series, 'gross_exposure': float, 'net_exposure': float}
strategies.composes_with: This is the retail-teaching primitive: it shows users how to BUILD a factor portfolio from a raw signal instead of calling a black box. Output weights.to_dict() -> simulate_rebalanced_portfolio for a backtest, then run_factor_regression on the resulting return stream to CONFIRM the realized value/size/momentum loading (closes the loop with the existing Fama-French engine). base_weights can come from any existing optimizer (e.g. tilt a max-diversification or risk-parity base toward quality). Composite scores feed straight in by averaging z-scores upstream.
references: Brandt, Santa-Clara & Valkanov (2009), 'Parametric Portfolio Policies: Exploiting Characteristics in the Cross-Section of Equity Returns', Review of Financial Studies 22(9):3411-3447
Asness, Frazzini & Pedersen (2019), 'Quality Minus Junk', Review of Accounting Studies 24:34-112
FTSE Russell, 'Portfolio Factor Allocation Schemes' (z-score tilt methodology), https://www.lseg.com/content/dam/ftse-russell/en_us/documents/research/portfolio-factor-allocation-schemes.pdf

strategies.name: Factor-mimicking / characteristic-sorted long-short portfolio
strategies.summary: Build a transparent single-factor long-short book by sorting the cross-section into top/bottom quantiles (or a cap-weighted high-minus-low spread) -- the same construction logic behind the SMB/HML/UMD factors your engine already consumes, but on the USER's own universe and signal.
strategies.math_core: Rank assets by the (signed) characteristic. Quantile construction: long the top q fraction (e.g. top 30%) equally or cap-weighted (+1/N_long each), short the bottom q (-1/N_short each) => dollar-neutral HML-style spread; portfolio return r_factor = mean(top) - mean(bottom). Alternatively the cross-sectional-regression factor (Fama-MacBeth / minimum-residual): r_factor,t = (X_t' X_t)^{-1} X_t' r_t for standardized exposure vector X_t, i.e. the unit-exposure minimum-variance characteristic portfolio. The breakpoints mirror Fama-French (30/70 NYSE breakpoints for HML/SMB); 2x3 double-sorts (e.g. size x value) reproduce SMB/HML construction. Beta/sector neutralization optional by regressing the long-short weights' exposures out.
strategies.key_params: characteristic: pd.Series; quantile=0.3 (top/bottom fraction; 0.1 for deciles); weighting='equal' (default) | 'cap' (needs market_caps); long_short=True (False => long-only top quantile); neutralize=None | 'sector' (needs group map) | 'market'; higher_is_better=True. Returns the long/short ticker sets and weights.
strategies.pitfalls: (1) Small universes make quantiles noisy (top 30% of 8 names = 2-3 stocks) -- guard a minimum count per leg. (2) Equal-weight legs overweight microcaps and create implicit size/illiquidity exposure -- offer cap weighting and disclose. (3) Survivorship/point-in-time on the characteristic and the universe (the caller owns timing/data). (4) Turnover: quantile membership flips at the boundary cause churn; consider buffer zones. (5) The long-short spread is a RETURN series, not a tradable price -- to compound it, model financing on the short leg; this primitive returns weights + the spread return, leaving cost assumptions explicit.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def factor_mimicking_portfolio(characteristic: pd.Series, returns: pd.DataFrame, quantile: float = 0.3, weighting: str = 'equal', long_short: bool = True, market_caps: pd.Series | None = None, higher_is_better: bool = True) -> dict:  # returns {'weights': pd.Series, 'long_tickers': list, 'short_tickers': list, 'factor_return': pd.Series, 'gross_exposure': float}
strategies.composes_with: Produces a self-built factor whose factor_return series can be APPENDED as a new column to the fetch_fama_french_factors DataFrame and fed to run_factor_regression / compute_factor_attribution -- letting users test a custom factor inside the existing attribution machinery, or attribute a portfolio to home-grown value/quality factors. weights.to_dict() -> simulate_rebalanced_portfolio backtests it. Stacks with characteristic_tilt_weights (continuous tilt) as the discrete-sort sibling, and the long leg alone can seed base_weights for an optimizer.
references: Fama & French (1993), 'Common Risk Factors in the Returns on Stocks and Bonds', J. Financial Economics 33:3-56
Fama & MacBeth (1973), 'Risk, Return, and Equilibrium: Empirical Tests', J. Political Economy 81(3):607-636
Kenneth French Data Library, factor construction details (https://mba.tuck.dartmouth.edu/pages/faculty/ken.french/data_library.html)

strategies.name: Factor-neutralization (residualization) of returns/exposures
strategies.summary: Strip unwanted factor exposure out of a portfolio or signal by regressing it on the Fama-French factors and keeping the residual -- e.g. make a momentum book market- and value-neutral, or isolate stock-specific alpha.
strategies.math_core: Given a return series r (portfolio or asset) and factor returns F (a subset of Mkt-RF, SMB, HML, RMW, CMA, UMD), regress r = a + B'F + e via OLS (reuse the existing run_factor_regression). The neutralized series is the residual plus intercept: r_neutral = a + e = r - B'F, which has ZERO loading on the chosen factors by construction. For NEUTRALIZING A SIGNAL/EXPOSURE cross-sectionally instead of a time series: regress the characteristic c (across stocks) on a sector/size design matrix and keep residuals so the tilt is sector- or size-neutral before weighting. Equivalent to hedging: short B_k units of factor k.
strategies.key_params: portfolio_returns: pd.Series; factor_returns: pd.DataFrame (from fetch_fama_french_factors); neutralize_factors=['Mkt-RF'] (which loadings to zero out; default market-neutral); keep_alpha=True (add intercept back so level is preserved); risk_free_rate=0.02. Returns the residualized return series plus the betas that were hedged out.
strategies.pitfalls: (1) In-sample betas are estimated with error, so realized exposure isn't exactly zero out-of-sample -- use rolling betas (run_rolling_factor_regression already exists) for time-varying hedges. (2) Neutralizing market beta does NOT make a long-short book risk-free; residual factor and idiosyncratic risk remain. (3) Overlapping/collinear factors (e.g. HML vs CMA) inflate beta variance -- neutralize a parsimonious set. (4) Residual returns are a synthetic series, not directly tradable without the implied factor hedge positions; surface the betas so the hedge is explicit. (5) Removing the market also removes the equity risk premium -- expect lower absolute return.
strategies.proposed_primitive: def factor_neutralize_returns(portfolio_returns: pd.Series, factor_returns: pd.DataFrame, neutralize_factors: list[str] = ['Mkt-RF'], keep_alpha: bool = True, risk_free_rate: float = 0.02) -> dict:  # returns {'neutral_returns': pd.Series, 'hedged_betas': pd.Series, 'residual_vol': float, 'r_squared': float}
strategies.composes_with: Thin wrapper over the EXISTING run_factor_regression (uses its 'betas'/'residuals'/'alpha' outputs directly), so it adds neutralization without new regression code. Output neutral_returns feeds calculate_metrics for clean alpha/Sharpe, and back into run_factor_regression to VERIFY the targeted loadings are ~0. Pairs with characteristic_tilt_weights / factor_mimicking_portfolio to turn a raw factor book into a market- or sector-neutral one; complements compute_factor_attribution by isolating the unexplained (alpha) sleeve.
references: Fama & French (2015), 'A Five-Factor Asset Pricing Model', J. Financial Economics 116(1):1-22
Grinold & Kahn (2000), 'Active Portfolio Management', 2nd ed., McGraw-Hill (factor hedging / residual return)
Carhart (1997), 'On Persistence in Mutual Fund Performance', J. Finance 52(1):57-82 (momentum factor)

auditSynth.module_boundaries: "Target: a thin orchestration layer over a headless analytics core. (1) report_context owns data fetch + the ONE set of shared inputs (price splits, mean_returns/cov_matrix/log_returns via get_optimization_inputs, resolved rfr, rebalance_freq). (2) A shared analytics core (currently scattered across metrics.py + opt_core.py + monte_carlo.py + attribution.py + factor_models.py) is the SOLE producer of every quantity: portfolio returns/growth (incl. rebalancing), moments, risk metrics, drawdown, VaR/CVaR, optimizer suite, factor regression, Monte Carlo — returning NUMERIC values, never display strings, on ONE return basis with ONE annualization constant. (3) *_report.py modules become pure assemblers: they pull from ctx and the core, then build sections/plots — they must NOT recompute returns, drawdown, rolling Sharpe, or re-run optimizers. (4) plotting modules receive already-computed numbers (e.g. draw the reported beta, not a refit OLS; consume stored port stats, not recompute via get_portfolio_stats). (5) combined_report stays a thin orchestrator but should expose the same knobs as standalones (MC params, actual overlay) and surface rather than swallow delegate failures. (6) Eliminate forked modules (monte_carlo_report vs monte_carlo) and shadowed duplicate defs (plot_correlation_heatmap)."
top_findings: DUAL RETURN BASIS is the single biggest latent bug: optimizers/frontier/risk-contribution/Monte-Carlo use LOG-return moments (opt_core.get_optimization_inputs:69, get_portfolio_stats:18-20) while the metrics grid uses SIMPLE pct_change vol/Sharpe (metrics.py:66,84) and geometric CAGR (:80). The SAME strategy shows different return/vol/Sharpe between the efficient-frontier star/risk plots and the metrics card. Fix: one canonical basis; make calculate_metrics consume ctx moments.

Centralize cumulative-growth + drawdown: 5+ growth reimplementations and 4 drawdown reimplementations on inconsistent bases mean the plotted underwater trough can disagree with the headline Max Drawdown (acute in optimization/validation where the chart uses get_portfolio_price growth but the scalar uses calculate_metrics cumprod). Add one growth helper + a calculate_max_drawdown that returns curve+scalar.

Kill the Monte Carlo fork: monte_carlo_report.py:13-185 duplicates ~90% of monte_carlo.py and they will diverge. Merge into one seeded engine, pass ctx.risk_free_rate into get_portfolio_stats (currently positional default 0.02), set np.random.seed (no seed today -> every run differs, stress != clean delta off base), and relabel simulated horizon VaR/CVaR distinctly from historical daily VaR/CVaR.

Stop discarding calculate_metrics.plot_data and stop the string round-trip: optimization_report.py:219 and validation_report throw away plot_data (`_`) then re-derive rolling Sharpe/drawdown/monthly by hand; validation_report._to_num:31-38 parses formatted '%' strings back to floats (documented past bug at :42-43). Make calculate_metrics return numerics + reuse its series.

Fix validation's denoising/optimizer inconsistency: compute_validation_analysis:144-146 optimizes on possibly-denoised ctx.cov_matrix while run_rolling_windows:92 recomputes RAW inputs (denoise default False). Same 'Min Vol'/'Max Sharpe' names optimized under different covariance treatments are shown side by side. Thread denoise settings through, and de-duplicate the optimizer suite shared with optimization_report.

Honor or remove rebalance_freq: it is plumbed end-to-end (combined_report.py:85 -> ctx) but never consumed by any compute_* (simulate_rebalanced_portfolio only imported in __init__). Either route portfolio growth through rebalancing.simulate_rebalanced_portfolio or drop the kwarg; today requesting a rebalance frequency silently no-ops.

Make combined a faithful, non-silent superset: forward MC params + actual-path overlay to compute_monte_carlo_analysis (currently called arg-less at combined_report.py:124, locked to 5000/252/$10k), remove the dead desc=False kwarg, and surface (not swallow) delegate exceptions (combined_report.py:99-126) so section completeness is deterministic.

Resolve factor/attribution correctness gaps: factor_report regresses SIMPLE returns of a renormalized Growth-of-$1 (alpha/beta/R² not comparable to other sections), runs two different OLS engines (statsmodels excess-return static vs hand-rolled raw-return rolling at :25-71), never threads ctx.risk_free_rate, and Brinson uses EQUAL bench_weights over the portfolio's own tickers (:219) instead of ctx.friendly_benchmark — so 'Total Active Return' is not measured against the stated benchmark.

researchSynth.namespace_proposal:
Keep the current FLAT single-package layout (src/quant_reporter/*.py, all symbols re-exported from __init__.py). Do NOT introduce subpackages like quant_reporter.backtest / .risk — that breaks the existing flat `from quant_reporter import X` contract and the house style where every module is one screen of pure functions. Instead add a small number of NEW flat modules and EXTEND three existing ones, grouped by dotted-name *convention* in docs only:

NEW modules (net-new files, mirror advanced_optimizers.py house style: numpydoc + References, validate-then-compute, logger.warning on edges, pandas-in/dict-out):
  • backtest.py        — portfolio_turnover, transaction_cost_model, generate_rebalance_dates, simulate_strategy, drawdown_stats, realized_tracking_error, apply_tax_lots  (the execution/measurement stack)
  • performance_stats.py — probabilistic_sharpe_ratio, deflated_sharpe_ratio, compare_strategies_oos  (honest OOS selection; upgrades validation_report.calculate_overfitting_score)
  • sizing.py           — target_volatility_scalar, forecast_portfolio_vol, kelly_leverage, kelly_weights, cppi_exposure, simulate_cppi, inverse_volatility_weights, risk_parity_overlay  (point-in-time sizers + path wrappers; the exposure-overlay layer)
  • signals.py          — compute_trailing_volatility, volatility_target_positions, time_series_momentum_signal, moving_average_crossover_signal, breakout_signal, cross_sectional_momentum_score, rank_to_long_short_weights, zscore_reversion_signal, build_spread, fit_ornstein_uhlenbeck, carry_score (+ dividend_carry/roll_yield_carry helpers), residual_momentum_score  (transparent score Series/DataFrame, never trades; lagged-safe)
  • robust_estimators.py — ledoit_wolf_covariance, james_stein_mean, resample_portfolio  (harden the (mean_returns, cov) inputs every optimizer already eats)
  • factor_tilts.py     — characteristic_tilt_weights, factor_mimicking_portfolio, factor_neutralize_returns  (build factor books on the user's own universe; close the loop with run_factor_regression)

EXTEND existing modules (no new file):
  • opt_core.py         — add risk_contributions(weights, cov) (extract the private helper already inside optimize_risk_parity) and optimize_min_cvar / portfolio_cvar (CVaR via scipy.optimize.linprog HiGHS — NO cvxpy, it is not a dep) and optimize_risk_budget (generalizes optimize_risk_parity to arbitrary budget b; the existing fn becomes the b=1/n case). These belong here because they are pure optimizer/risk-model primitives alongside MVO and validate_covariance_matrix.
  • metrics.py          — drawdown_stats can live here as a sibling of calculate_max_drawdown (which it calls); but house it in backtest.py if you prefer to keep metrics.py minimal. portfolio_cvar wraps the existing calculate_var_cvar.
  • validation_report.py — refactor run_rolling_windows to ALSO return per-window weight dicts (currently computed then discarded), exposing a target_weight_schedule that simulate_strategy consumes.

CANONICAL EXAMPLE STRATEGIES to ship (one example_*.py per, matching examples/ pattern, using ctx-based API): (1) example_walk_forward_backtest.py — run_rolling_windows -> target_weight_schedule -> simulate_strategy(cost_model=...) -> compare_strategies_oos with DSR/PSR vs Equal/MinVol/MaxSharpe (the flagship 'honest OOS' demo). (2) example_robust_inputs.py — ledoit_wolf_covariance + james_stein_mean -> find_optimal_portfolio, side-by-side vs raw sample inputs (shows estimation error). (3) example_trend_following.py — time_series_momentum_signal + moving_average_crossover_signal ensemble -> volatility_target_positions -> simulate_strategy (the CTA sleeve). (4) example_factor_tilt.py — cross_sectional_momentum_score / characteristic_tilt_weights -> simulate_rebalanced_portfolio -> run_factor_regression to confirm realized loadings (closes the factor loop). (5) example_risk_overlays.py — vol-targeting + CPPI stacked on a base optimizer, with drawdown_stats before/after.

All symbols re-exported from __init__.py in new sections mirroring the existing comment-banner grouping (e.g. '# --- Backtest & Execution ---', '# --- Risk Overlays & Sizing ---', '# --- Tactical Signals ---', '# --- Robust Estimators ---', '# --- Factor Tilts ---'). recommend=True opinion-pickers (half-Kelly, 10% target vol, CPPI m=5, sensible cost model from ADV) live in a thin separate wrapper layer, never inside the primitives.
top_additions: volatility_target_positions + compute_trailing_volatility (signals.py) — THE single highest-leverage primitive: the shared signal->weights bridge that EVERY trend/carry/reversion signal and the vol-targeting overlay depend on. Nothing in the tactical or sizing families works without it. Ship first.

simulate_strategy + portfolio_turnover + transaction_cost_model (backtest.py) — the cost-aware engine that wraps (not replaces) simulate_rebalanced_portfolio, emits a trade blotter, and makes every backtest number (turnover, cost drag) a callable primitive. Unlocks the entire backtest/tax/OOS stack and is the bridge from optimizer output to measured strategy.

ledoit_wolf_covariance (robust_estimators.py) — one shrinkage call hardens the cov input of ALL seven existing optimizers + Black-Litterman simultaneously; guaranteed PD so it removes most regularize_covariance fallbacks. Highest value-per-line in the whole roadmap; zero new deps (sklearn already pinned).

compare_strategies_oos + deflated/probabilistic Sharpe (performance_stats.py) — the honest-selection cap that directly upgrades the existing calculate_overfitting_score heuristic to a statistically grounded DSR/PSR. This is the institutional 'no black box' differentiator and the answer to backtest overfitting.

generate_rebalance_dates + drawdown_stats (backtest.py) — generate_rebalance_dates is a back-compatible drop-in for the inline freq block AND adds threshold/band rebalancing; drawdown_stats is pure post-processing usable on ANY wealth series (incl. the existing benchmark) and feeds the existing plot_drawdown_comparison. Both are low-effort, high-reach, retrofit onto current backtests with no engine change.

target_volatility_scalar + kelly_leverage + cppi_exposure (sizing.py) — the exposure-overlay trio that stacks on any base weight vector; vol-targeting alone is the most-used institutional risk control and demonstrates primitive composition (inverse_volatility_weights -> target_volatility_scalar).

cross_sectional_momentum_score + characteristic_tilt_weights (signals.py / factor_tilts.py) — strongest composition story in the repo: a score Series feeds find_optimal_portfolio as mean_returns OR Black-Litterman view_dict OR a long-only tilt, all with EXISTING machinery. The retail-teaching centerpiece (build a factor portfolio, don't call a black box).

james_stein_mean (robust_estimators.py) — fixes the single biggest failure of max-Sharpe (garbage expected returns); the view-free lightweight sibling of Black-Litterman, stacks directly after ledoit_wolf_covariance to harden both optimizer inputs.

PITFALL: brutally sensitive to mu error (overbetting punished worse than under) — default fractional + REQUIRE shrunk mu in docs; always cap leverage; regularize ill-conditioned Sigma; recommend=True picks half-Kelly, never the primitive.

roadmap.value: Prosumer->PM: Michaud resampling -> stable diversified weights that don't lurch on tiny input changes; per-asset dispersion gives an uncertainty band on the SAME efficient-frontier plot (calculate_efficient_frontier_curve). PITFALL: prefer block bootstrap over parametric Normal (fat tails/serial corr); reduces allocation VARIANCE not mu BIAS (Scherer) — compose with shrinkage, don't substitute; B*optimizer is the runtime cost — reuse the SLSQP path, parallelize if needed.

roadmap.signature: def characteristic_tilt_weights(characteristic: pd.Series, base_weights: pd.Series|None=None, tilt_strength=1.0, mode='long_short', winsorize=3.0, higher_is_better=True) -> dict  # {'weights','z_scores','gross_exposure','net_exposure'}
roadmap.depends_on: base_weights can come from any optimizer; output -> simulate_rebalanced_portfolio -> run_factor_regression to CONFIRM realized loading (closes the factor loop)
roadmap.value: All tiers, the retail-teaching primitive: BUILD a factor portfolio from a raw signal instead of calling a black box; composite scores feed in by averaging z-scores upstream (Brandt-Santa-Clara-Valkanov parametric policy). PITFALL: caller owns point-in-time timing (lag fundamentals); winsorize or one outlier hijacks the book; long-only exp-tilt isn't scale-free — clip/cap per-name; dollar-neutral != beta-neutral (pair with factor_neutralize_returns).

roadmap.signature: def factor_mimicking_portfolio(characteristic: pd.Series, returns: pd.DataFrame, quantile=0.3, weighting='equal', long_short=True, market_caps: pd.Series|None=None, higher_is_better=True) -> dict  # {'weights','long_tickers','short_tickers','factor_return','gross_exposure'}
roadmap.depends_on: factor_return series can be APPENDED as a column to fetch_fama_french_factors and fed to run_factor_regression / compute_factor_attribution; discrete-sort sibling of characteristic_tilt_weights
roadmap.value: Prosumer->PM: build a transparent SMB/HML/UMD-style long-short factor on the USER's own universe and signal, then test it inside the EXISTING attribution machinery; long leg alone can seed an optimizer's base_weights. PITFALL: guard a minimum count per leg (top 30% of 8 names = 2-3 stocks); equal-weight overweights microcaps -> offer cap weighting and disclose; the spread is a RETURN series not a tradable price — model short-leg financing to compound, leave cost assumptions explicit.

roadmap.signature: def factor_neutralize_returns(portfolio_returns: pd.Series, factor_returns: pd.DataFrame, neutralize_factors=['Mkt-RF'], keep_alpha=True, risk_free_rate=0.02) -> dict  # {'neutral_returns','hedged_betas','residual_vol','r_squared'}
roadmap.net_new_or_extends: net-new (factor_tilts.py); THIN wrapper over existing run_factor_regression
roadmap.depends_on: factor_models.run_factor_regression (uses its alpha/betas/residuals outputs directly — neutral = alpha + residuals); fetch_fama_french_factors; run_rolling_factor_regression for time-varying hedges
roadmap.value: Prosumer->PM: strip unwanted factor exposure (make a momentum book market/value-neutral; isolate stock-specific alpha) with NO new regression code; output feeds calculate_metrics for clean alpha and back into run_factor_regression to VERIFY ~0 loadings. PITFALL: in-sample betas -> realized exposure not exactly zero OOS (use rolling betas); neutralizing market removes the equity premium (lower absolute return); residual returns need the implied hedge positions to be tradable — surface hedged_betas; neutralize a parsimonious factor set (collinearity inflates beta variance).

roadmap.signature: def residual_momentum_score(returns, factor_returns, lookback=252, skip=21, estimation_window=252, standardize=True) -> pd.DataFrame
roadmap.net_new_or_extends: net-new (signals.py); reuses run_factor_regression-style OLS from factor_models
roadmap.depends_on: factor_models.fetch_fama_french_factors + run_factor_regression; drop-in replacement for cross_sectional_momentum_score in every composition (optimizer/BL/tilt)
roadmap.value: Prosumer->PM: cleaner less crash-prone momentum (~2x historical Sharpe of total-return momentum) with near-zero factor betas; natural partner to the existing Fama-French attribution report. PITFALL: rolling per-asset regressions are slow — vectorize/cache; needs aligned FF data (reuse repo's, mind calendar/timezone); residualizing removes a real premium you may have wanted (deliberate neutralization, not free alpha); still has non-zero crash risk.

roadmap.signature: def fit_ornstein_uhlenbeck(series, dt=1.0, price_transform='log', min_obs=60) -> dict  # {theta,mu,sigma,half_life,equilibrium_std,ar1_beta,is_mean_reverting}
roadmap.depends_on: none (OLS/AR(1)); feeds zscore_reversion_signal (set window ~ k*half_life, bands from equilibrium_std); pairs with build_spread
roadmap.value: Prosumer: model-based reversion speed/half-life so reversion windows are ESTIMATED not guessed — turns the heuristic z-score signal into a model-grounded one; deterministic -> testable against a simulated OU path with known theta. PITFALL: if AR(1) b>=1 or <=0 the series is NOT mean-reverting and half-life is undefined — GUARD and flag, never return garbage; assumes constant theta/mu over window; calibrating on a non-stationary series gives confidently-wrong params (the most common failure); estimates params only, decides no trades.

roadmap.signature: def compare_strategies_oos(returns_dict, benchmark_returns=None, n_trials=None, sr_threshold=0.0, periods_per_year=252) -> dict  # {'summary','sharpe_diff_pvalues','best_by_dsr'}; def probabilistic_sharpe_ratio(returns, sr_threshold=0.0, periods_per_year=252) -> float; def deflated_sharpe_ratio(returns, n_trials, periods_per_year=252) -> float
roadmap.depends_on: OOS wealth/returns from multiple simulate_strategy runs (one per optimizer/cost assumption) built on run_rolling_windows splits; PSR/DSR are independently surfaceable report primitives
roadmap.value: Prosumer->PM, the honest-selection cap and institutional 'no black box' differentiator: ranks strategies by overfitting-aware DSR/PSR + HAC Ledoit-Wolf Sharpe-difference p-values, not raw OOS Sharpe (the single most common backtest sin). PITFALL: n_trials must count EVERY config tried (incl. abandoned) or the deflation is defeated; strategies must share a COMMON OOS window/frequency; overlapping walk-forward windows violate iid -> use HAC/Ledoit-Wolf SEs not the naive t-test; uses scipy.stats + statsmodels HAC (both deps present).

roadmap.signature: def realized_tracking_error(portfolio_returns, benchmark_returns, portfolio_weights=None, benchmark_weights=None, ex_ante_cov=None, periods_per_year=252) -> dict  # {'tracking_error','information_ratio','active_share','ex_ante_te'}
roadmap.net_new_or_extends: net-new (backtest.py); complements calculate_metrics without duplicating it
roadmap.depends_on: runs on simulate_strategy output vs ReportContext.friendly_benchmark; reuses cov_matrix from get_optimization_inputs for ex-ante TE
roadmap.value: Prosumer->PM: standard 'how active was I and was it worth it' panel (ex-post TE, IR, Cremers-Petajisto active share, optional ex-ante TE); reuses the benchmark already in ReportContext. PITFALL: label ex-post vs ex-ante TE (they routinely disagree); active share needs ALIGNED same-universe weights (benchmark reindexed onto the union, missing=0) or it's overstated; IR sign-flips when mean active return ~ 0.

roadmap.signature: def apply_tax_lots(blotter, prices, method='FIFO', st_rate=0.37, lt_rate=0.20, wash_sale=False) -> dict  # {'realized_gains','tax_drag','lot_ledger','after_tax_wealth'}
roadmap.net_new_or_extends: net-new (backtest.py); strictly downstream of simulate_strategy, independent of optimizers
roadmap.depends_on: simulate_strategy blotter; after_tax_wealth re-runs through drawdown_stats / calculate_metrics for pre- vs post-tax comparison
roadmap.value: PM / private-wealth differentiator: after-tax wealth curve with FIFO/LIFO/HIFO lot selection + optional wash-sale flagging, exposing the full per-lot ledger (no black box). PITFALL: track qty in SHARES not weights (float basis error accumulates over long backtests); wash-sale rules are US-centric and complex — ship a DOCUMENTED approximation and WARN, don't silently mis-state; decision-support NOT tax advice — must be labeled.

researchSynth.notes:
SHARED-DEPENDENCY MAP (verified against the actual code, not just the research): (1) opt_core.validate_covariance_matrix + regularize_covariance are the universal cov-guard — already imported by all four advanced_optimizers; every new cov-consuming primitive (CVaR, risk-budget, vol-targeting portfolio scaling, Kelly, resampling) must route through them. (2) compute_trailing_volatility is the single estimator shared by ALL tactical signals AND the vol-targeting/risk-parity overlays — it is the de-facto vol estimator the research's family-notes keep referencing; build it once in signals.py and import it into sizing.py. (3) ledoit_wolf_covariance is the meta-dependency: it hardens the (mu,Sigma) for every optimizer, Black-Litterman, AND the inner draws of resample_portfolio — the cleanest single 'estimator' upgrade. (4) factor_models.run_factor_regression already returns EXACTLY {alpha,betas,r_squared,residuals,factor_pvalues,summary} (verified by reading the source), so factor_neutralize_returns (neutral=alpha+residuals) and residual_momentum_score are genuinely THIN reuses — no new regression code, confirming their effort ratings.

THE ONE UNLOCK: refactor validation_report.run_rolling_windows to RETURN its per-window weight dicts. Right now (lines 102-118) it builds Equal/MinVol/MaxSharpe/User weight dicts every window and then THROWS THEM AWAY, keeping only OOS Sharpe. Exposing those as a target_weight_schedule is the keystone: it is the missing input to simulate_strategy, which feeds the blotter -> transaction_cost_model + apply_tax_lots + drawdown_stats + realized_tracking_error, whose OOS outputs feed compare_strategies_oos. That single change converts the library from 'optimize + one-shot simulate' to a full walk-forward, cost-aware, honestly-evaluated backtest engine — the highest-leverage structural move in the roadmap. It exactly parallels the second discard the research flags: simulate_rebalanced_portfolio computes drifted weights at lines 92-95 and discards them — portfolio_turnover just keeps them. Both 'compute-then-discard' sites are where the institutional measurement layer was always latent in the code.

CROSS-CUTTING CONVENTIONS to enforce (from reading the code): (a) ANNUALIZATION DOUBLE-COUNT is the #1 silent bug — get_optimization_inputs already does cov*252 / mean*252; any sklearn/scenario primitive must fit on the same periodicity and annualize once with the SAME factor. Centralize the x252 in the returns-in/annualized-out primitives (ledoit_wolf_covariance, optimize_min_cvar). (b) metrics.calculate_metrics returns FORMATTED STRINGS (f\"{x:.2%}\"), and validation_report already has a _to_num shim to undo that — so ALL new primitives must return plain floats/Series/dicts and must NOT duplicate calculate_metrics; compare_strategies_oos/realized_tracking_error/drawdown_stats compute numerics directly. (c) NO new dependencies are needed and NONE should be added: sklearn>=1.0, scipy, statsmodels>=0.13, requests are pinned in pyproject. Specifically CVaR must use scipy.optimize.linprog (HiGHS) — cvxpy is NOT a dependency and must not be introduced. (d) Weights are np.ndarray (ticker order) in optimizers but dict in rebalancing/reports — simulate_strategy must accept BOTH (dict one-shot, DataFrame schedule) so it bridges optimizer output without an adapter; this is the load-bearing back-compat design choice. (e) recommend=True is the home for all opinions (half-Kelly, 10% target vol, CPPI m=5, cost model from ADV) — primitives stay opinion-free with explicit params.

LOOK-AHEAD is the dominant correctness risk across signals AND backtest: every rolling stat uses trailing-only windows, every signal is lagged >=1 period before touching returns, and schedule rows are decided on data up to d-1, traded at d. Bake this into property tests (hypothesis is already the test extra). BACK-COMPAT WATCH: simulate_rebalanced_portfolio currently returns a (Series, DataFrame) tuple AND simulate_rebalanced_portfolio is the only rebalancing export in __init__.py — do not change its signature; simulate_strategy is the additive superset.

PHASING RATIONALE: Phase 1 ships the five pure foundations (compute_trailing_volatility, volatility_target_positions, ledoit_wolf_covariance, portfolio_turnover, drawdown_stats) that everything else imports; Phase 2 is the cost-aware engine + honest OOS (the flagship: simulate_strategy + transaction_cost_model + generate_rebalance_dates + compare_strategies_oos, plus the run_rolling_windows weight-schedule refactor); Phases 3-6 layer overlays, advanced estimation, tactical signals, and factor tilts that all reuse Phase-1 primitives; Phase 7 (tax) is the optional private-wealth capstone. The four effort-L items (simulate_strategy, compare_strategies_oos, apply_tax_lots, residual_momentum_score) are the only ones needing a dedicated test session each. SINGLE ADDITION THAT UNLOCKS THE MOST: simulate_strategy — it is the hub every backtest/tax/tracking/OOS primitive plugs into and the bridge between the existing optimizers and measured performance; volatility_target_positions is the equivalent hub for the entire tactical-signals family.
